Professor View 01 / 28
Business English -- 5 Aulas

Patricia Ruffo

De "sobreviver em inglês" a defender suas opiniões com confiança nas reuniões internacionais

B1+ São Paulo, SP Nutricionista Clínica / Pesquisadora Abbott 60 min / Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Patricia Ruffo
50 anos
Nutricionista Clínica / Pesquisadora Abbott
São Paulo, SP
B1+ (Intermediário Alto)
Business English -- Argumentação Científica
5 aulas de 60 min
Online (Zoom)

Jornada de Transformação

De: Participante passiva em conferências, insegura para se posicionar em inglês nas reuniões da Abbott

Para: Debatedora científica ativa, capaz de apresentar dados, defender opiniões e questionar pesquisas com confiança em contextos internacionais

Promessa do Programa

"De 'sobreviver em inglês' a defender suas opiniões com confiança nas reuniões internacionais"

Forças

  • Vocabulário técnico sólido em nutrição e pesquisa clínica
  • Alta tolerância ao erro -- não tem medo de tentar
  • Autoconsciência linguística -- sabe onde precisa melhorar
  • Motivação intrínseca forte -- carreira depende do inglês
  • Fluência em leitura de artigos científicos em inglês

Pontos de Melhoria

  • Argumentação oral -- dificuldade para estruturar opiniões em tempo real
  • Pronúncia de termos técnicos e conectores
  • Confusão entre tempos verbais (present simple vs present perfect)
  • Participação passiva em discussões -- ouve mais do que fala
  • Velocidade de compreensão oral em discussões rápidas

Currículo -- 5 Aulas

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
1Who Is Patricia Ruffo? -- Diagnostic + Self-IntroductionPresent simple + Present perfectConference role-playRecord 2-min self-introduction
2Holding the Floor -- Opinions & DisagreementFirst conditional + Opinion framesMeeting disagreement role-playWrite disagreement email
3Smart Questions -- Conference DiscussionsSecond conditional + Past simple vs Present perfectQ&A simulationWrite conference questions
4Reporting & Updating -- Team PresentationsReported speechProject update briefingWrite project update email
5Full Simulation -- Abbott Science SummitAll structures integrated35-min immersion simulationFinal reflection audio

Welcome, Patricia!

Onboarding

Bem-vinda ao programa! Antes de cada aula, complete as atividades de Pre-class. Aqui estão 5 frases de emergência que você pode usar a qualquer momento:

1 Could you repeat that, please? Poderia repetir, por favor?
2 I am not sure I understand. Could you explain? Não tenho certeza se entendi. Poderia explicar?
3 Let me think about that for a moment. Deixe-me pensar um momento.
4 That is a great question. Essa é uma ótima pergunta.
5 In my experience, I would say... Na minha experiência, eu diria...
Aula 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Patricia Ruffo?

Diagnostic + Self-Introduction: Present simple & Present perfect
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Introduce -- to tell someone your name or present a topic
"Let me introduce myself. I am Patricia Ruffo."
Collaborate -- to work together with someone on a project
"I collaborate with universities on clinical research."
Responsible -- having the duty to manage or control something
"I am responsible for the nutrition research program."
Currently -- at the present time; now
"I am currently working on a new clinical trial."
Clinical Research -- scientific studies that test new treatments on people
"Clinical research is essential for developing new treatments."
Functional Ingredients -- food components that provide health benefits beyond nutrition
"We study functional ingredients for glycemic control."
Glycemic Control -- managing blood sugar levels in the body
"Glycemic control is critical for patients with diabetes."
Trial -- a formal test of something new, especially a medical treatment
"The trial showed promising results for the new supplement."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Introduce
Collaborate
Responsible
Currently
Clinical Research
Functional Ingredients
Glycemic Control
Trial

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Patricia is a clinical nutritionist. She works at Abbott in São Paulo. Every day, she reviews research data and collaborates with universities. She has worked in clinical research for over twenty years. She has published several papers on glycemic control. Currently, she is responsible for a new trial on functional ingredients. She has collaborated with researchers from five different countries. Patricia speaks Portuguese and studies English to present her findings at international conferences.

1. What does Patricia do every day?
A She has published papers.
B She reviews research data and collaborates with universities.
C She has worked for twenty years.
2. Which sentence describes an achievement (something completed at an unspecified time)?
A She works at Abbott in São Paulo.
B She reviews research data every day.
C She has published several papers on glycemic control.
3. Why does Patricia study English?
A To present her findings at international conferences.
B To collaborate with Abbott in São Paulo.
C To review research data every day.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple = facts, habits, routines (coisas que acontecem regularmente ou são verdades)
Present Perfect = experiences, achievements, actions connected to now (experiências, conquistas, ações com conexão ao presente)

TenseStructureExample
Present SimpleSubject + verb (base/s)"She works at Abbott."
Present PerfectSubject + have/has + past participle"She has worked there for 20 years."

Common Mistake:

"I work here since 2015."

"I have worked here since 2015."

Use Present Perfect com "since" e "for" para indicar duração até o presente.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or verb form.

"Let me myself at the conference."
"I with researchers from five countries."
"She is for the clinical trial."
"I am working on glycemic control research."
"The showed promising results."
"We study for better nutrition."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "I am responsible for clinical research at Abbott."
? "Good morning, everyone. Let me introduce myself."
? "I am happy to answer any questions about my work."
? "I have been collaborating with universities for over ten years."
? "My name is Patricia Ruffo, and I am a clinical nutritionist."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

I am a clinical researcher at Abbott, based in São Paulo.
I am currently working on glycemic control with functional ingredients.
I have been collaborating with universities for over ten years.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are at an ASPEN conference. A researcher asks: "What do you do?" What is the best response?
A "I am Patricia. I like nutrition."
B "I am a clinical nutritionist at Abbott. I focus on glycemic control research."
C "I have a job in São Paulo."
2. A colleague says: "How long have you been working in clinical research?" What is the correct answer?
A "I work in clinical research since 2005."
B "I worked in clinical research for twenty years."
C "I have been working in clinical research for over twenty years."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are at an ASPEN conference. A researcher from Japan asks: "Could you tell me about your current work?" Record your answer in English. Try to include: your name, your role, what you are currently working on, and one achievement.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1 Let me introduce myself. I am Patricia Ruffo. Deixe-me me apresentar. Eu sou Patricia Ruffo.
2 I am a clinical nutritionist at Abbott. Sou nutricionista clínica na Abbott.
3 I have been working in clinical research for over twenty years. Trabalho em pesquisa clínica há mais de vinte anos.
4 I am currently focused on glycemic control. Estou atualmente focada em controle glicêmico.
5 I would love to discuss this further. Adoraria discutir isso mais a fundo.
Aula 02 -- Pre-class

Holding the Floor

Opinions & Disagreement: First conditional & Opinion frames
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Disagree -- to have a different opinion from someone else
"I disagree with the proposed changes."
Evidence -- facts or information that show something is true
"The evidence supports our hypothesis."
Perspective -- a particular way of looking at something
"From my perspective, the data is clear."
Outcome -- the result of an action or situation
"The outcome of the trial was positive."
Propose -- to suggest an idea for people to consider
"I propose we extend the deadline."
Convince -- to make someone agree or believe something
"The results will convince the committee."
Approach -- a way of dealing with a problem
"We need a different approach to this problem."
Significant -- important enough to have an effect
"The improvement was significant."
Acknowledge -- to accept that something is true
"I acknowledge that the timeline is tight."
Counterargument -- reasons against an opinion
"That is a strong counterargument."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Disagree
Evidence
Perspective
Outcome
Propose
Convince
Approach
Significant
Acknowledge
Counterargument

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Patricia disagrees with Dr. Santos about the supplement formula. She acknowledges his perspective, but she believes the current approach is showing significant progress. In her opinion, the evidence from the last six months supports her position. She proposes extending the trial: "If we change the formula now, we will lose valuable data." Her counterargument is strong: "If the results do not improve in three months, I will agree to a new approach." The outcome of the debate depends on who convinces the committee first.

1. What does Patricia disagree about?
A The supplement formula changes
B The meeting time
C The research budget
2. What does Patricia propose?
A Cancel the trial
B Extend the trial for three more months
C Change the formula immediately
3. Which sentence uses the first conditional correctly?
A "If we change the formula, we will lose data."
B "If we will change the formula, we lose data."
C "If we changed the formula, we lost data."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

First Conditional = real possibilities in the future (possibilidades reais no futuro)
Opinion Frames = phrases to introduce opinions politely (frases para introduzir opiniões educadamente)

StructureFormulaExample
First ConditionalIf + present simple, will + base form"If we change the formula, the results will improve."
Opinion FrameFrame + opinion"In my opinion, we should continue."

5 Opinion Frames

  • "In my opinion,..." / "Na minha opinião,..."
  • "I see your point, but..." / "Entendo seu ponto, mas..."
  • "Based on the evidence,..." / "Com base nas evidências,..."
  • "I would argue that..." / "Eu argumentaria que..."
  • "From my perspective,..." / "Da minha perspectiva,..."

Common Mistake:

"If we will change the formula, the results will improve."

"If we change the formula, the results will improve."

Depois de "if", use o presente simples -- NUNCA "will".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"I see your point, but I with this approach."
"Based on the , the outcome was significant."
"If we a new approach, the committee will approve it."
"I that there are limitations in our study."
"Her was very convincing."
"From my , we need more evidence."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "If we change the formula now, we will lose six months of data."
? "However, I would argue that we need more evidence."
? "I propose that we extend the trial for three more months."
? "I see your point, Dr. Santos."
? "Based on the data, our current approach is showing progress."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

I see your point, but I would argue that we need more evidence before making changes.
If we change the formula, the results will improve significantly.
From my perspective, we should acknowledge the limitations first.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are at an Abbott meeting. Dr. Santos proposes changing the formula. You disagree. What is the best response?
A "No, that is wrong."
B "I see your point, but based on the evidence, I would argue that we should continue the current approach."
C "I do not care about the formula."
2. A colleague asks: "What will happen if we change the formula?" What is the correct answer using first conditional?
A "If we will change the formula, we lose data."
B "If we changed the formula, we would lose data."
C "If we change the formula, we will lose six months of valuable data."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are at an Abbott team meeting. A colleague proposes a change you disagree with. Record your response in English. Try to include: an opinion frame, a first conditional sentence, and at least 3 vocabulary words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1 I see your point, but I would like to propose an alternative. Entendo seu ponto, mas gostaria de propor uma alternativa.
2 Based on the evidence, I disagree with this approach. Com base nas evidências, discordo dessa abordagem.
3 If we change the methodology, the outcomes will be more significant. Se mudarmos a metodologia, os resultados serão mais significativos.
4 From my perspective, we should acknowledge the limitations first. Da minha perspectiva, devemos reconhecer as limitações primeiro.
5 I understand your counterargument, but the data suggests otherwise. Entendo seu contra-argumento, mas os dados sugerem o contrário.
Aula 03 -- Pre-class

Smart Questions

Conference Discussions: Second conditional & Past simple vs Present perfect
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Hypothesis -- a proposed explanation that can be tested
"Our hypothesis is that functional ingredients reduce glycemic response."
Methodology -- the system of methods used in a study
"The methodology of this study was peer-reviewed."
Correlation -- a connection between two things
"There is a strong correlation between diet and glycemic control."
Preliminary -- coming before the main part; initial
"The preliminary results are very promising."
Replicate -- to repeat a study to confirm results
"We need to replicate this study with a larger sample."
Variable -- a factor that can change in an experiment
"The main variable in our experiment was the dosage."
Sample -- a group chosen to represent a larger population
"Our sample included 120 participants from three countries."
Bias -- a tendency to favor one thing over another unfairly
"We must eliminate bias from our research design."
Peer-reviewed -- evaluated by experts in the same field
"This study was published in a peer-reviewed journal."
Implication -- the possible effect or result of something
"The implications of our findings are significant for public health."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Hypothesis
Methodology
Correlation
Preliminary
Replicate
Variable
Sample
Bias
Peer-reviewed
Implication

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Dr. Nakamura presented his findings at the ASPEN conference last Tuesday. He has studied nutritional supplements for fifteen years. His team published a paper on correlation between diet and glycemic control in 2024. They have replicated the study twice with different samples. The preliminary results showed a significant connection. Patricia asked smart questions about the methodology and potential bias. She has always been curious about the implications of nutritional research. If she were leading this study, she would use a larger sample to test the hypothesis. If they had more funding, they would replicate the experiment with 500 participants.

1. Which sentence uses the Past Simple for a specific time?
A "Dr. Nakamura presented his findings last Tuesday."
B "He has studied supplements for fifteen years."
C "They have replicated the study twice."
2. Which sentence uses the Second Conditional?
A "If she were leading this study, she would use a larger sample."
B "She asked smart questions about the methodology."
C "He has studied nutritional supplements."
3. What has Patricia always been curious about?
A "The implications of nutritional research."
B "The methodology of the study."
C "The preliminary results."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Second Conditional = situações hipotéticas ou irreais (hypothetical or unreal situations)
Past Simple vs Present Perfect = revisão aprofundada (deepened review)

StructureFormulaExample
Second ConditionalIf + past simple, would + base form"If we had a larger sample, the results would be more reliable."
Past SimpleSubject + verb (past) + specific time"We published the results last year."
Present PerfectSubject + have/has + past participle"We have published three papers on this topic."

5 Smart Question Frames

  • "Could you elaborate on...?" / "Poderia detalhar...?"
  • "Have you considered the possibility that...?" / "Você considerou a possibilidade de que...?"
  • "If you were to..., what would...?" / "Se você fosse..., o que...?"
  • "What are the implications of...?" / "Quais são as implicações de...?"
  • "Is there a correlation between...?" / "Há uma correlação entre...?"

Common Mistake:

"If we would have a larger sample, the results would be more reliable."

"If we had a larger sample, the results would be more reliable."

Depois de "if" no second conditional, use o passado simples -- NUNCA "would".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"The was supported by the preliminary data."
"If we this study, the results would be more significant."
"There is a strong between the two variables."
"We must eliminate from our sample selection."
"The of this research are far-reaching."
"Our was published in a peer-reviewed journal."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "Could you elaborate on how you selected your sample?"
? "What are the implications of your findings for clinical practice?"
? "Thank you for your presentation, Dr. Nakamura."
? "If you were to replicate this study, what would you change?"
? "I have a question about your methodology."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

Could you elaborate on the methodology of your study?
If we had a larger sample, the results would be more reliable.
What are the implications of your findings for public health?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are at a conference Q&A. A researcher presents weak methodology. What is the best question to ask?
A "Your methodology is wrong."
B "Could you elaborate on your methodology? Have you considered the possibility that the sample size could introduce bias?"
C "I do not understand your research."
2. A colleague asks: "What would happen if we used a different variable?" What is the correct response using second conditional?
A "If we will use a different variable, the results change."
B "If we used a different variable, we would see different outcomes."
C "If we use a different variable, we will see different outcomes."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are at the ASPEN conference. Dr. Nakamura just presented his research on nutritional supplements. Record yourself asking 3 smart questions. Try to use: at least 2 question frames, 1 second conditional sentence, and 3 vocabulary words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1 Could you elaborate on your methodology? Poderia detalhar sua metodologia?
2 Have you considered the possibility that there is bias in the sample? Você considerou a possibilidade de viés na amostra?
3 If you were to replicate this study, what would you change? Se você fosse replicar este estudo, o que mudaria?
4 What are the implications of your preliminary findings? Quais são as implicações dos seus resultados preliminares?
5 Is there a correlation between the variables you studied? Há uma correlação entre as variáveis que você estudou?
Aula 04 -- Pre-class

Reporting & Updating

Team Presentations: Reported speech
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Report -- to give information about something officially
"I need to report our findings to the team."
Update -- to give someone the latest information
"Let me update you on the latest trial results."
Summarize -- to give a short statement of the main points
"I will summarize the key points of our research."
Highlight -- to draw attention to something important
"I would like to highlight three important milestones."
Deadline -- the latest time by which something must be done
"The deadline for the final report is next Friday."
Progress -- movement toward a goal
"We have made significant progress this quarter."
Stakeholder -- a person with interest in a project
"The stakeholders are expecting a detailed update."
Milestone -- an important event in development
"We reached an important milestone last month."
Deliverable -- something that must be provided as part of a project
"The deliverable is due by the end of the week."
Feedback -- comments about how well someone is doing
"The feedback from the committee was very positive."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Report
Update
Summarize
Highlight
Deadline
Progress
Stakeholder
Milestone
Deliverable
Feedback

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last week, Patricia reported to her team that they had completed the first phase of the clinical trial. She highlighted three important milestones and summarized the progress so far. Dr. Nakamura said that the correlation between dosage and glycemic response was significant. He mentioned that the sample size needed to be larger. Patricia told the stakeholders that the next deliverable was a comprehensive progress report. She updated the team on the deadline: December 15th. The feedback from the committee was very positive. Patricia said: ‘If we continue at this pace, we will reach our next milestone by November.’
1. What did Dr. Nakamura say about the correlation?
A "He said that the correlation was significant."
B "He said the correlation is insignificant."
C "He mentioned there was no correlation."
2. Which sentence uses reported speech correctly?
A "Patricia reports that they complete phase one."
B "Patricia reported that they had completed phase one."
C "Patricia reported that they have completed phase one."
3. What is the deadline for the final deliverable?
A "November 30th."
B "December 15th."
C "January 10th."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip: Reported Speech

Grammar

When we report what someone said, we shift the verb tense back (backshift).

Direct Speech Reported Speech Example
Present simple Past simple “The results are good.” → She said the results were good.
Present perfect Past perfect “We have completed it.” → She said they had completed it.
Will Would “I will present.” → He said he would present.
Can Could “We can finish.” → They said they could finish.
Reporting Verbs / Verbos de Relato
  • said (that) / disse (que)
  • told (someone) that / disse a (alguém) que
  • mentioned (that) / mencionou (que)
  • reported (that) / relatou (que)
Common Mistake:
Wrong: “He told that we need more data.”
Right: “He told us that we needed more data.”
‘Told’ sempre precisa de um objeto (told ME/US/HER). ‘Said’ nunca tem objeto direto de pessoa.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I need to our progress to the stakeholders."
"She that the deadline was next Friday."
"The team that they had reached an important milestone."
"Let me the key deliverables for this quarter."
"He us that the feedback from the committee was positive."
"I would like to the progress we have made."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "Dr. Nakamura reported that the preliminary results were significant."
? "I will summarize the key points and send my feedback by the deadline."
? "Good morning, everyone. I would like to update you on our project."
? "The next deliverable is due by December 15th."
? "Last month, we reached an important milestone."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

I would like to report that we have completed the first milestone.
Dr. Nakamura said that the preliminary results were significant.
The stakeholders mentioned that they expected a progress update by the deadline.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
Your director asks you to report on a project. What is the best response?
A "I would like to report that we have made significant progress this quarter. We reached an important milestone last month."
B "The project is fine, I think we are doing okay."
C "I don't have any updates right now."
Convert to reported speech: "The results are promising."
A "She said that the results are promising."
B "She said that the results were promising."
C "She told the results were promising."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you need to give a project update to your stakeholders. Record yourself reporting on a recent project: mention at least 2 milestones, 1 deadline, and use reported speech to quote a colleague. Try to use at least 5 vocabulary words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1 I would like to report that we have made significant progress. Gostaria de relatar que fizemos um progresso significativo.
2 Let me update you on the latest developments. Deixe-me atualizá-lo sobre os últimos desenvolvimentos.
3 Dr. Nakamura said that the results were very promising. Dr. Nakamura disse que os resultados foram muito promissores.
4 The team mentioned that they would need more time to finish the deliverable. A equipe mencionou que precisaria de mais tempo para terminar a entrega.
5 I will summarize the key milestones and send my feedback by the deadline. Vou resumir os principais marcos e enviar meu feedback até o prazo.
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

Abbott Science Summit

Full Simulation: All structures integrated
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this final lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Panel -- a group of experts who discuss a topic publicly
“Patricia was invited to join the panel discussion.”
Keynote -- the main speech at a conference or event
“The keynote speaker presented groundbreaking research.”
Moderator -- a person who leads a discussion or debate
“The moderator asked each delegate to share their findings.”
Consensus -- a general agreement among a group
“The team reached a consensus on the new methodology.”
Delegate -- a person chosen to represent a group at a conference
“Every delegate received a copy of the conference abstract.”
Networking -- the process of meeting people to exchange information and build professional relationships
“Networking at the symposium led to a new collaboration.”
Symposium -- a formal meeting where experts present and discuss a specific topic
“The symposium featured presentations from twelve countries.”
Abstract -- a short summary of a research paper or presentation
“Her abstract was accepted for the main session.”
Findings -- the results of research or investigation
“The findings of our study have important implications.”
Recommendation -- a suggestion about the best course of action
“My recommendation is to replicate the study with a larger sample.”

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Panel
Keynote
Moderator
Consensus
Delegate
Networking
Symposium
Abstract
Findings
Recommendation

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the text and answer the questions.

The Abbott Science Summit is the most important symposium for nutritional research. Patricia has been preparing her keynote for weeks. She presented her abstract to the panel last month. The moderator said that her findings were groundbreaking. If Patricia were the only delegate from Brazil, she would feel nervous, but three colleagues are also attending. She reported that the team had reached a consensus on the recommendation for future studies. Dr. Santos mentioned that he disagreed with her methodology, but Patricia proposed that they discuss it at the panel. If the delegates approve the recommendation, the symposium will fund a larger study. Networking at the summit has led to collaborations with researchers from five countries.
1. Which grammar structure is used in “If Patricia were the only delegate, she would feel nervous”?
A First Conditional (real possibility)
B Second Conditional (hypothetical situation)
C Reported Speech (backshift)
2. In “She reported that the team had reached a consensus,” which reporting verb is used?
A said
B told
C reported
3. “If the delegates approve the recommendation, the symposium will fund a larger study” is an example of:
A First Conditional (real future possibility)
B Second Conditional (hypothetical)
C Present Perfect

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Program Review

Grammar

Review of all grammar structures from the program.

Lesson Structure Key Phrases
1 Present Simple / Perfect I work... / I have worked...
2 First Conditional + Opinion Frames If we change... / I see your point...
3 Second Conditional + Question Frames If we had... / Could you elaborate...?
4 Reported Speech She said that... / He told us that...
5 All Integrated Summit simulation
Your Complete Toolkit

Opinion Frames:
  • I see your point, but...
  • In my opinion...
  • From my perspective...
  • I agree with you on that.
  • I respectfully disagree because...
Question Frames:
  • Could you elaborate on...?
  • What do you mean by...?
  • How does that compare to...?
  • Would you mind explaining...?
  • Could you give an example of...?
Reporting Verbs:
  • said (that) — general reporting
  • told (someone) that — reporting to a specific person
  • mentioned (that) — casual or brief reporting
  • reported (that) — formal reporting

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

“The discussed the findings of the latest study.”
“The speaker highlighted the importance of peer-reviewed research.”
“The asked each delegate to present their abstract.”
“We reached a on the recommendation for future studies.”
“ at the symposium helped us find new collaborators.”
“Her were presented at the keynote session.”

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put these sentences in the correct order for a summit presentation.

? “Based on the evidence, my recommendation is to continue the current approach.”
? “I would like to present our findings on glycemic control to the panel.”
? “I would like to thank the moderator and all the delegates for their feedback.”
? “Good morning, everyone. Welcome to the Abbott Science Summit.”
? “My recommendation is that we reach a consensus on the methodology.”

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

I would like to present our findings to the panel.
The delegates reached a consensus on the next steps.
The delegates at the symposium discussed the implications of our research.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
You are at an international symposium and want to present your research findings to the panel. Which is the most appropriate opening?
A “I would like to present our findings on glycemic control to the panel.”
B “So here are the results, everyone.”
C “I have some things to talk about.”
After a productive discussion, the moderator asks for a closing remark. Which response integrates reported speech and a recommendation?
A “That was great, thanks everyone.”
B “Dr. Santos mentioned that he disagreed with the methodology. My recommendation is that we reach a consensus on the next steps.”
C “I think we should just move on.”

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Record a 2-minute presentation as if you are at the Abbott Science Summit. Integrate: self-introduction, your research findings, an opinion frame, a question for the audience, and a recommendation. This is your final challenge -- show everything you have learned!

Survival Card -- Lesson 5 (FINAL)

1 I would like to present our findings to the panel. Gostaria de apresentar nossos resultados ao painel.
2 Based on the evidence, my recommendation is to continue the current approach. Com base nas evidências, minha recomendação é continuar a abordagem atual.
3 The delegates reached a consensus on the next steps. Os delegados chegaram a um consenso sobre os próximos passos.
4 If we had more funding, we would replicate the study at a larger symposium. Se tivéssemos mais financiamento, replicaríamos o estudo em um simpósio maior.
5 I would like to thank the moderator and all the delegates for their feedback. Gostaria de agradecer ao moderador e a todos os delegados pelo feedback.
Aula 06 -- Pre-class

Finding Common Ground -- Diplomatic Negotiation

Negotiating like a diplomat: softening your message, making concessions and reaching an agreement. New vocabulary: compromise, concession, flexible, reasonable, soften, tentative, reservation, standpoint, middle ground, reach an agreement.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Compromise -- an agreement where each side gives up something
"We reached a fair compromise on the deadline." (Acordo / meio-termo)
Concession -- something you agree to give up to help an agreement
"As a concession, we can extend the timeline." (Concessão)
Flexible -- willing to change or adapt to other people's needs
"I am flexible on the budget, but not on the data." (Flexível)
Reasonable -- fair, sensible and easy to accept
"Your request is perfectly reasonable." (Razoável)
Soften -- to make a message gentler and less direct
"I soften my opinion so it sounds polite." (Suavizar)
Tentative -- not final yet; still open to change
"We have a tentative plan for the launch." (Provisório)
Reservation -- a doubt or concern about a decision
"I have one reservation about the timeline." (Ressalva / reserva)
Standpoint -- the position from which you see a topic
"From a scientific standpoint, we need more data." (Ponto de vista)
Middle ground -- a solution that is acceptable to both sides
"Let us find some middle ground together." (Meio-termo)
Reach an agreement -- to finally decide on something everyone accepts
"I am sure we can reach an agreement today." (Chegar a um acordo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Compromise
Concession
Flexible
Reasonable
Soften
Tentative
Reservation
Standpoint
Middle ground
Reach an agreement

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

In the project meeting, Patricia wants to disagree without sounding rude, so she decides to soften her message. From a scientific standpoint, she has one reservation about the timeline. Instead of saying "No," she says, "I might suggest a different approach," and "Perhaps we could split the project into two phases." She is flexible on the budget, and she offers a concession on the launch date. The marketing team thinks her plan is reasonable, even though the schedule is still tentative. Both sides look for some middle ground, and after a short discussion they finally reach an agreement. A good compromise protects the science and keeps most of the original plan.

1. Why does Patricia choose to soften her message?
A She wants to disagree without sounding rude.
B She wants to cancel the whole project.
C She agrees completely with the marketing team.
2. What does she offer the marketing team?
A She offers nothing and refuses to change.
B She is flexible on the budget and offers a concession on the launch date.
C She offers to leave the company.
3. How does the meeting end?
A Both sides leave angry, with no decision.
B They find middle ground and reach an agreement.
C They decide to start the whole project again.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Softening with modals

GRAMMAR

Em negociações, suavizamos as ideias com modais e advérbios para soar diplomático. / In negotiations, we soften ideas with modals and adverbs to sound diplomatic.

Function / FunçãoSoftening structure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Make a suggestionwe could / perhaps we could + base verb"Perhaps we could split it into two phases."
Soften an opinionI might / I would say + idea"I would say the data is still tentative."
Raise a concernI have one reservation about ..."I have one reservation about the timeline."
Concede a pointThat said, / On the other hand, ..."That said, I am flexible on the budget."

Key point / Ponto-chave: Em inglês de negócios, "No" direto soa rude. Use could, might, perhaps e would para suavizar, e marcadores como That said ou On the other hand para fazer concessões e chegar a um meio-termo.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Perhaps we could find a fair ."
"From a scientific , we need more data."
"I am on the budget."
"I have one about the timeline."
"Your request is perfectly ."
"I am sure we can today."

Stage 2: Put the Negotiation Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a diplomatic negotiation in the correct order.

?After that, propose a possible compromise.
?First, acknowledge the other person's point of view.
?Finally, confirm the agreement and the next steps.
?Next, raise your own concern in a soft and polite way.
?Then, ask for the other side's input.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Perhaps we could find some middle ground.
Talvez pudéssemos encontrar um meio-termo.
From a scientific standpoint, I have one reservation.
De um ponto de vista científico, tenho uma ressalva.
I am flexible, but your request must be reasonable.
Eu sou flexível, mas seu pedido precisa ser razoável.
That said, I am happy to make a small concession.
Dito isso, fico feliz em fazer uma pequena concessão.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A colleague proposes a deadline you cannot meet. You want to disagree politely. You say:
A "I see your point, but perhaps we could move the date by one week."
B "No. That date is impossible and wrong."
C "You never understand the science."
You want to offer something to help the agreement. You say:
A "I will not change anything at all."
B "As a concession, I am flexible on the budget."
C "Concession the budget flexible I am."
You want to close the discussion positively. You say:
A "Agreement reach we maybe can later."
B "I am confident we can reach an agreement that works for both teams."
C "This meeting was a complete waste of time."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a colleague wants to launch your study results too early. Negotiate diplomatically: acknowledge their point, soften your concern, offer one concession, and try to reach an agreement. Use at least three of today's words and one softening structure (could, might, perhaps, that said).

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1I see your point, but perhaps we could try another approach.Entendo seu ponto, mas talvez pudéssemos tentar outra abordagem.
2From a scientific standpoint, I have one reservation.De um ponto de vista científico, tenho uma ressalva.
3As a concession, I am flexible on the deadline.Como concessão, sou flexível quanto ao prazo.
4Let us find some middle ground.Vamos encontrar um meio-termo.
5I am confident we can reach an agreement.Estou confiante de que podemos chegar a um acordo.
Aula 07 -- Pre-class

Making the Case -- Persuasion & Cause and Effect

Building a convincing business argument with cause-and-effect language. New vocabulary: proof, therefore, lead to, factor, impact, demonstrate, conclude, rationale.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Proof -- a fact or piece of data that shows something is true
"The trial gives us clear proof that the formula works." (Prova / evidência)
Therefore -- for that reason; introduces a result
"The data is strong, therefore we should invest." (Portanto)
Lead to -- to cause or produce a particular result
"A bigger budget would lead to a bigger study." (Levar a / resultar em)
Factor -- one of the things that influences a result
"Cost is the main factor in this decision." (Fator)
Impact -- the strong effect that something has on a result
"The new formula has a real impact on patient care." (Impacto)
Demonstrate -- to show something clearly with facts or examples
"The data demonstrate that the treatment is effective." (Demonstrar)
Conclude -- to end an argument with a final decision or point
"To conclude, I recommend phase two." (Concluir)
Rationale -- the set of reasons that explain a decision
"My rationale is simple: the proof is strong." (Justificativa / raciocínio)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Proof
Therefore
Lead to
Factor
Impact
Demonstrate
Conclude
Rationale

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the steering committee, Patricia wants to make a convincing case for phase two. She starts with the proof: in the last trial, the new formula demonstrated a clear impact on glycemic control. The main factor behind her recommendation is the consistency of the data. Because the results are so strong, she argues that a larger budget would lead to a larger study, and a larger study would lead to even more reliable results. Her rationale is simple. The data are clear, therefore the investment makes sense. As a result, the committee is convinced. To conclude, Patricia recommends that they approve phase two before the end of the quarter.

1. What does Patricia start her case with?
A The proof from the last trial.
B A list of her personal opinions.
C A request to cancel the project.
2. According to her, what would a larger budget lead to?
A A smaller, faster study.
B A larger study with more reliable results.
C Less work for the team.
3. How does the committee react?
A They reject the proposal completely.
B They are convinced and she recommends approving phase two.
C They ask her to leave the company.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Cause and effect

GRAMMAR

Para argumentar, conectamos causas e efeitos com conectores. / To argue, we connect causes and effects with linking words.

Function / FunçãoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Give the causebecause + clause"Because the data is strong, we will invest."
Give the result..., therefore / as a result + clause"The data is strong, therefore we will invest."
Connect cause to effectcause + lead to + noun"More funding will lead to a bigger study."
Show with evidencethe data demonstrate (that) ..."The data demonstrate a clear impact."

Key point / Ponto-chave: Use because para a causa, e therefore ou as a result para o efeito. Use lead to + substantivo (nunca + verbo no infinitivo) para conectar uma causa diretamente a um resultado.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The trial gives us strong ."
"The data is strong, we should invest."
"A bigger budget would a bigger study."
"Cost is the main in this decision."
"The data a clear impact."
"To , I recommend phase two."

Stage 2: Put the Argument Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a convincing business case in the correct order.

?After that, explain the cause and effect of the results.
?First, state your recommendation clearly.
?Finally, conclude and ask for a decision.
?Next, present the proof and the main data points.
?Then, give your rationale in one short sentence.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

The data demonstrate a clear impact on patient care.
Os dados demonstram um impacto claro no cuidado do paciente.
Because the proof is strong, therefore we should invest.
Como a prova é forte, portanto deveríamos investir.
A bigger budget would lead to a bigger study.
Um orçamento maior levaria a um estudo maior.
My rationale is simple, and the main factor is the proof.
Minha justificativa é simples, e o principal fator é a prova.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to present strong evidence to the committee. You say:
A "The trial gives us clear proof, and the data demonstrate a real impact."
B "I just feel that this is a good idea."
C "Proof the data impact demonstrate clear."
You want to connect a cause to its effect. You say:
A "More funding will lead to grow the study."
B "More funding would lead to a bigger study."
C "More funding because a bigger study therefore."
You want to close your argument and ask for a decision. You say:
A "Conclude I phase two recommend therefore."
B "To conclude, my rationale is clear, therefore I recommend phase two."
C "I have nothing else to say about this."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are pitching your study to the committee. Make a convincing case: state your recommendation, present your proof, explain the impact using because and therefore, and conclude with your rationale. Use at least three of today's words and one cause-and-effect connector (because, therefore, as a result, lead to).

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1The data demonstrate a clear impact.Os dados demonstram um impacto claro.
2Because the proof is strong, we should invest.Como a prova é forte, deveríamos investir.
3A bigger budget would lead to a bigger study.Um orçamento maior levaria a um estudo maior.
4My rationale is simple, therefore I recommend phase two.Minha justificativa é simples, portanto recomendo a fase dois.
5To conclude, the main factor is the consistent proof.Para concluir, o principal fator é a prova consistente.
Aula 08 -- Pre-class

Questioning Research -- Critical Appraisal & Hedging

Reading a study like a reviewer: judging the study design, raising queries and hedging your conclusions. New vocabulary: study design, cohort, conflict of interest, limitation, reliable, assumption, query, plausible.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Study design -- the set of methods used to design and run a study
"The study design is clear and well described." (Desenho do estudo)
Cohort -- the group of people or items chosen to be studied
"The cohort is quite small, only forty participants." (Coorte)
Conflict of interest -- an unfair influence that makes results less reliable
"There may be conflict of interest because the company funded it." (Conflito de interesse)
Limitation -- a weak point that reduces what a study can prove
"The main limitation is the short follow-up." (Limitação)
Reliable -- trustworthy; giving consistent, dependable results
"The study design itself is reliable." (Confiável)
Assumption -- something we accept as true without testing it
"The study makes one strong assumption about diet." (Suposição / pressuposto)
Query -- a question or point of doubt you want to raise
"My main query is about the cohort size." (Dúvida / questionamento)
Plausible -- believable and reasonable, but not yet proven
"The findings are plausible rather than proven." (Plausível)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Study design
Cohort
Conflict of interest
Limitation
Reliable
Assumption
Query
Plausible

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the journal club, Patricia reviews a new study. The study design is clear, so she says it is reliable. However, the cohort is small, so the data only suggests an effect; it does not prove one. She also notices one assumption the authors do not test, and she raises a polite query about a possible conflict of interest, because the company funded the work. The main limitation, she says, appears to be the short follow-up. The treatment seems to help, and the results may be useful, but she is careful: the findings are plausible rather than proven.

1. Why does Patricia say the data only suggests an effect?
A Because the study design is weak and unreliable.
B Because the cohort is small, so the result is not proven.
C Because she did not read the study.
2. What query does she raise?
A A possible conflict of interest, because the company funded the work.
B Whether the authors used a computer.
C How long the journal club lasted.
3. What is her overall verdict?
A The findings are completely proven and certain.
B The findings are plausible rather than proven.
C The study should be deleted entirely.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Hedging your claims

GRAMMAR

Na ciência, afirmações fortes demais soam descuidadas. Suavizamos com hedging para soar honesto. / In science, claims that are too strong sound careless. We hedge to sound honest.

Function / FunçãoHedging structure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Report a findingthe data suggests + noun"The data suggests a positive effect."
Give an impressionit / the result seems + adjective"The result seems reliable."
Express possibilitymay / might + base verb"These results may not apply to everyone."
Make an observationappears to + base verb"The treatment appears to help."

Key point / Ponto-chave: Em inglês científico, evite "this proves". Use suggests, seems, may e appears to para suavizar. Depois de may e might use o verbo base (sem to); só appears leva to.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The is clear and well described."
"The is quite small, only forty participants."
"There may be because the company funded it."
"The main is the short follow-up."
"My main is about the cohort size."
"The findings are rather than proven."

Stage 2: Put the Appraisal Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a critical appraisal in the correct order.

?After that, raise your main query about the cohort or the data.
?First, summarize what the study set out to show.
?Finally, give a balanced verdict using hedging language.
?Next, comment on the strengths of the study design.
?Then, point out the most important limitation.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

The data suggests a positive effect.
Os dados sugerem um efeito positivo.
My main query is about the cohort size.
Minha principal dúvida é sobre o tamanho da amostra.
The study design seems reliable, but there may be some conflict of interest.
A metodologia parece confiável, mas pode haver algum viés.
I would say the findings are plausible rather than proven.
Eu diria que os achados são plausíveis, mas não comprovados.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A study reports a positive result, but the cohort is small. You want to comment carefully. You say:
A "The data suggests a positive effect, but the result may not apply to everyone."
B "This proves the product works for all people, certainly."
C "The study is useless and completely wrong."
You want to raise a polite query about a possible conflict of interest. You say:
A "Query conflict of interest the company funded is."
B "My main query is about a possible conflict of interest, as the company funded the study."
C "You are clearly lying about your data."
You want to give a balanced final verdict. You say:
A "Proven everything is, no questions at all."
B "The study design seems reliable, so I would say the findings are plausible rather than proven."
C "I refuse to say anything about this study."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you just read a study with a small cohort and industry funding. Appraise it: comment on the study design, raise one query, name the main limitation, and give a balanced verdict. Use at least three of today's words and one hedging structure (suggests, seems, may, appears to).

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1The data suggests a positive effect, but it may not apply to everyone.Os dados sugerem um efeito positivo, mas pode não valer para todos.
2My main query is about the cohort, which seems quite small.Minha principal dúvida é sobre a amostra, que parece bem pequena.
3The study design seems reliable, but there may be some conflict of interest.A metodologia parece confiável, mas pode haver algum viés.
4The main limitation appears to be the short follow-up.A principal limitação parece ser o acompanhamento curto.
5I would say the findings are plausible rather than proven.Eu diria que os achados são plausíveis, mas não comprovados.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Describing Data, Trends & Charts

Telling the story behind the numbers: comparatives and trend language to describe charts, increases, declines and peaks. New vocabulary: trend, increase, decline, peak, plateau, fluctuate, proportion, axis.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Trend -- the general direction in which data moves over time
"The overall trend is clearly positive." (Tendência)
Increase -- a rise; when a value goes up
"We saw a steady increase in the first quarter." (Aumento)
Decline -- a fall; when a value goes down over time
"The control group showed a slow decline." (Queda / declínio)
Peak -- the highest point that a value reaches
"The marker reached a clear peak in month four." (Pico)
Plateau -- a period when a value stays almost flat after rising
"After the peak, the values reached a plateau." (Estabilização / platô)
Fluctuate -- to go up and down in an irregular way
"The figures fluctuate a little from week to week." (Oscilar)
Proportion -- a part of a whole, often shown as a percentage
"The proportion of responders rose to seventy percent." (Proporção)
Axis -- a reference line on a chart (horizontal or vertical)
"The vertical axis shows the average score." (Eixo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Trend
Increase
Decline
Peak
Plateau
Fluctuate
Proportion
Axis

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the team meeting, Patricia presents the results of a six-month study. She first explains the chart: the horizontal axis shows the months, and the vertical axis shows the average score. The overall trend is very positive. In the first quarter there was a sharp increase, and the marker reached a clear peak in month four. After that, the values did not fall; they reached a stable plateau. The numbers fluctuate a little from week to week, which is normal. The control group, however, showed a slow decline, so the treatment line is much higher than the control. The most important point is the proportion of responders, which rose from forty to seventy percent.

1. What does the vertical axis show?
A The months of the study.
B The average score for each group.
C The name of the patients.
2. What happened after the peak in month four?
A The values reached a stable plateau.
B The values fell to zero immediately.
C The study was cancelled.
3. How does the treatment group compare with the control group?
A They are exactly the same.
C The control group is higher.
B The treatment line is much higher than the control.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparatives & trend language

GRAMMAR

Para descrever dados, comparamos valores e descrevemos o movimento da linha. / To describe data, we compare values and describe how the line moves.

Function / FunçãoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Compare two (short)adj + -er + than"Group A is higher than group B."
Compare two (long)more + adj + than"It was more significant than the control."
Top of allthe + -est / the most + adj"Month four shows the highest value."
Describe the movementverb + adverb (sharply, slightly)"The score rose sharply / fell slightly."

Key point / Ponto-chave: Adjetivos curtos usam -er than e the -est; adjetivos longos usam more ... than e the most .... Nunca combine os dois (errado: more higher). Use advérbios como sharply, steadily e slightly para dizer o quanto o dado se moveu.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The overall is clearly positive."
"The marker reached a clear in month four."
"After the peak, the values reached a stable ."
"The control group showed a slow ."
"A high of patients responded well."
"The vertical shows the average score."

Stage 2: Put the Chart Description in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of describing a chart in the correct order.

?After that, point out the highest peak.
?First, introduce the chart and explain the two axes.
?Finally, highlight the most important proportion or figure.
?Next, describe the overall trend in one sentence.
?Then, mention any decline or plateau.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

The overall trend is clearly positive.
A tendência geral é claramente positiva.
It rose sharply to a peak and then reached a plateau.
Subiu acentuadamente até um pico e depois estabilizou.
The treatment group is much higher than the control.
O grupo de tratamento está bem mais alto que o controle.
The vertical axis shows the average score.
O eixo vertical mostra a pontuação média.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A line goes up fast and then stays flat. You describe it. You say:
A "It rose sharply to a peak and then reached a plateau."
B "The line did a high and after stop go up."
C "It is more higher than before."
You compare the treatment group with the control group. You say:
A "The treatment is more higher than the control."
B "The treatment group is much higher than the control."
C "Treatment control higher the than is."
You want to introduce the chart to your audience. You say:
A "Numbers here are the chart of things."
B "The horizontal axis shows the months and the vertical axis shows the score."
C "This chart no need explanation."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are presenting a chart from one of your own Abbott studies. Introduce the two axes, describe the overall trend in one sentence, point out the peak and the plateau, and compare two groups. Use at least four of today's words and one comparative (higher than, the highest).

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1The horizontal axis shows the months and the vertical axis shows the score.O eixo horizontal mostra os meses e o eixo vertical mostra a pontuação.
2The overall trend is clearly positive.A tendência geral é claramente positiva.
3It rose sharply to a peak and then reached a plateau.Subiu acentuadamente até um pico e depois estabilizou.
4The treatment group is much higher than the control.O grupo de tratamento está bem mais alto que o controle.
5A high proportion of patients responded well.Uma alta proporção de pacientes respondeu bem.
Aula 09 -- Pre-class

Defending Your Data Under Pressure

Answering tough questions calmly: clarifying your data, conceding a fair point and standing by your robust result. New vocabulary: clarify, recognize, stand by, rebuttal, anecdotal, robust, address, scrutiny.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Clarify -- to explain something so it is easier to understand
"Let me clarify what the data actually shows." (Esclarecer)
Recognize -- to accept that a point or fact is true or fair
"I recognize the concern about the control group." (Reconhecer)
Stand by -- to keep supporting and defending something you said
"I stand by the central finding of the study." (Manter / sustentar)
Rebuttal -- a reply that argues against a criticism
"Her rebuttal answered every objection clearly." (Réplica / refutação)
Anecdotal -- based on personal stories, not on careful research
"That claim is anecdotal, not statistical." (Anedótico / casual)
Robust -- strong, reliable and hard to disprove
"Our result is robust across every test." (Robusto / sólido)
Address -- to deal with a problem or concern directly
"We addressed that concern in the analysis." (Tratar / abordar)
Scrutiny -- careful and critical examination of something
"The data held up under heavy scrutiny." (Escrutínio / exame rigoroso)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Clarify
Recognize
Stand by
Rebuttal
Anecdotal
Robust
Address
Scrutiny

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

During the Q&A, a reviewer challenges Patricia. First, she stays calm and decides to clarify exactly what the data shows. She does not get defensive. Instead, she chooses to recognize the fair point about the control group, and she explains that the team already addressed it in the supplementary analysis. The reviewer says the evidence sounds anecdotal, but Patricia has a strong rebuttal: the result is robust and held up under repeated scrutiny. So while she concedes that more long-term data is needed, she still decides to stand by the central conclusion, because it agrees with two independent studies.

1. What does Patricia do first under pressure?
A She stays calm and clarifies what the data shows.
B She gets defensive and refuses to answer.
C She agrees that the whole study is wrong.
2. How does she respond to the "anecdotal" comment?
A She says nothing and changes the subject.
B She gives a rebuttal: the result is robust and held up under scrutiny.
C She admits the result was just luck.
3. What does she concede, and what does she stand by?
A She concedes everything and stands by nothing.
B She concedes more data is needed, but stands by the central conclusion.
C She stands by the sample size and ignores the conclusion.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Concede, then stand firm

GRAMMAR

Sob pressão, conceda um ponto justo e depois sustente o resultado central. / Under pressure, concede a fair point and then stand firm on your core result.

Move / MovimentoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Concede a fair pointI recognize / I concede that + clause"I recognize the concern about the sample."
Concede with a clauseWhile it is true (that) ..."While it is true the data is limited, ..."
Turn and contrastbut / however / that said"..., however the result is robust."
Stand by the coreI stand by + noun"I stand by the central finding."

Key point / Ponto-chave: A resposta mais forte sob pressão é conceder + contrastar + defender. Use I recognize ou I concede that + oração (nunca infinitivo), vire com but, however ou that said, e então stand by o resultado central.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let me what the data actually shows."
"I the concern about the control group."
"Our result is across every test."
"That claim is , not statistical."
"The data held up under heavy ."
"I the central finding of the study."

Stage 2: Put the Defending Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of defending your data in the correct order.

?After that, concede any fair and minor point.
?First, recognize the question and thank the person for it.
?Finally, offer to share the dataset for further scrutiny.
?Next, clarify exactly what your data shows.
?Then, give your rebuttal and stand by the central finding.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Let me clarify what the data actually shows.
Deixe-me esclarecer o que os dados realmente mostram.
I recognize the concern, but I stand by the finding.
Reconheço a preocupação, mas sustento a conclusão.
That claim is anecdotal, but our result is robust.
Essa afirmação é anedótica, mas nosso resultado é robusto.
The conclusion will survive further scrutiny.
A conclusão vai resistir a um exame mais rigoroso.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A reviewer says your sample is too small. You want to concede a fair point but stay firm. You say:
A "I recognize the concern, but the result held up under scrutiny."
B "You clearly do not understand my research."
C "Fine, the whole study is probably wrong."
Someone calls your evidence anecdotal. You want a calm rebuttal. You say:
A "Anecdotal evidence robust is the my data."
B "My rebuttal is simple: the result is robust and we addressed that point."
C "I never said the data was good anyway."
You want to close your answer by inviting more review. You say:
A "Scrutiny the dataset you can never see."
B "I am happy to share the full dataset so you can review the methods."
C "There is no need to check anything else."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a reviewer attacks your study during a Q&A, calling your evidence anecdotal. Defend your data calmly: clarify the result, recognize one fair point, give your rebuttal, and stand by the core finding. Use at least three of today's words and the concede + contrast + defend structure.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1Let me clarify what the data actually shows.Deixe-me esclarecer o que os dados realmente mostram.
2I recognize the concern, but I stand by the finding.Reconheço a preocupação, mas sustento a conclusão.
3That claim is anecdotal; our result is robust.Essa afirmação é anedótica; nosso resultado é robusto.
4We addressed that point in the analysis.Nós tratamos desse ponto na análise.
5The conclusion will survive further scrutiny.A conclusão vai resistir a um exame mais rigoroso.
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

Review -- The Scientific Debate

Block 2 review: persuasion, questioning research and defending data, brought together in a mock panel debate. New vocabulary: debate, motion, counterpoint, moderator, rebut, articulate, persuasive, valid.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Debate -- a formal discussion where two sides argue opposite opinions
"The debate about the new study lasted an hour." (Debate)
Motion -- the statement or topic that the debate is about
"The motion is that the study should be published now." (Moção / tema)
Counterpoint -- an opposite argument that answers your point
"She raised a strong counterpoint about the sample size." (Contra-argumento)
Moderator -- the person who leads a debate and keeps it fair
"The moderator gave each speaker two minutes." (Mediador)
Rebut -- to answer an argument and show why it is wrong
"He used new data to rebut the counterpoint." (Refutar / rebater)
Articulate -- to express your ideas clearly and in good order
"She can articulate her main argument in one sentence." (Articular / expressar com clareza)
Persuasive -- able to convince other people that you are right
"Her case was clear, calm and very persuasive." (Persuasivo / convincente)
Valid -- logical, sound and supported by good evidence
"Your point is valid because the data supports it." (Válido)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Debate
Motion
Counterpoint
Moderator
Rebut
Articulate
Persuasive
Valid

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the Abbott science panel, the moderator reads the motion and reminds everyone of the rules. Patricia speaks first, and she is careful to articulate her main argument in one clear sentence. The evidence is strong, therefore her case is persuasive. Then a colleague raises a counterpoint: he says the sample may be too small. Patricia does not get angry; she stays calm and decides to rebut the point with new data from a larger study. Because the data supports her, the argument is still valid. This is the secret of a good debate: you question the data, not the person, and you support every claim with evidence.

1. What does the moderator do at the start?
A Reads the motion and reminds everyone of the rules.
B Argues strongly for one side.
C Decides who wins before anyone speaks.
2. How does Patricia answer the counterpoint?
A She attacks her colleague and refuses to listen.
B She stays calm and rebuts the point with new data.
C She agrees and gives up her argument.
3. According to the text, what is the secret of a good debate?
A Speak louder than the other side.
B Question the data, not the person, and support every claim with evidence.
C Never let the other side speak.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Language of debate (Block 2 review)

GRAMMAR

No debate, juntamos as estruturas do Bloco 2: causa e efeito para persuadir, hedging para questionar e marcadores para defender. / In a debate, we combine the Block 2 structures: cause and effect to persuade, hedging to question, and markers to defend.

Function / FunçãoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Persuade (cause & effect)..., therefore ... / ... leads to ..."The evidence is strong, therefore the result is valid."
Question research (hedging)It seems / it may / it suggests ..."It seems the sample may be too small."
Rebut a counterpointLet me rebut that ..."Let me rebut that with new data."
Defend / concedeI acknowledge ..., but I stand by ..."I acknowledge your point, but I stand by my data."

Key point / Ponto-chave: No debate científico, você articula um argumento persuasivo, ouve o counterpoint, e o rebate com evidência para mostrar que o ponto é válido. Questione os dados, nunca a pessoa. / In a scientific debate, you articulate a persuasive argument, hear the counterpoint, and rebut it with evidence to show the point is valid. Question the data, never the person.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"As the , I will keep the debate fair."
"The is that we should publish the study now."
"That is a fair about the sample."
"Let me that point with new data."
"Her case was clear and very ."
"The point is because the data supports it."

Stage 2: Put the Debate Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a scientific debate in the correct order.

?After that, the other team offers a counterpoint and questions the data.
?First, the moderator reads the motion and explains the rules.
?Finally, the judge gives feedback and decides which case was more persuasive.
?Next, the first team articulates its argument and gives the evidence.
?Then, each side has a chance to rebut and to clarify.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

As the moderator, I will read the motion for today.
Como mediador, vou ler a moção de hoje.
Let me articulate my main argument in one sentence.
Deixe-me articular meu argumento principal em uma frase.
That is a fair counterpoint, and I would like to rebut it.
Esse é um contra-argumento justo, e eu gostaria de refutá-lo.
My point is valid because the data supports it.
Meu ponto é válido porque os dados o sustentam.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A colleague says your sample is too small. You want to rebut the counterpoint politely. You say:
A "That is a fair counterpoint. Let me rebut it: a larger study found the same result."
B "You are wrong and you do not understand science."
C "Counterpoint your small sample wrong is."
You want to make your case in a persuasive way. You say:
A "I think maybe it is true, but I am not sure."
B "The evidence is strong, therefore the result is valid."
C "Valid the result because strong evidence."
You are the moderator and you want to open the debate. You say:
A "I will argue for the first team today."
B "As your moderator, I will read the motion and give each speaker equal time."
C "The debate is over before it starts."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are in a scientific debate about your own research. Articulate one persuasive argument with evidence, then answer a counterpoint by rebutting it calmly. Use at least three of today's words and one Block 2 structure (therefore, it seems, I acknowledge but I stand by).

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1As your moderator, the motion for today is the following.Como mediador, a moção de hoje é a seguinte.
2Let me articulate my main argument in one sentence.Deixe-me articular meu argumento principal em uma frase.
3That is a fair counterpoint. Let me rebut it.Esse é um contra-argumento justo. Deixe-me refutá-lo.
4My point is valid because the data supports it.Meu ponto é válido porque os dados o sustentam.
5I am confident our case is the more persuasive one.Estou confiante de que nosso caso é o mais persuasivo.
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Structuring a Scientific Presentation -- Signposting

Organizing a talk so the audience always knows where you are: signposting with firstly, moving on and to summarize. New vocabulary: outline, agenda, transition, emphasize, recap, takeaway, framework, overview.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Outline -- a short plan showing the main parts of a talk
"Let me start with a quick outline of my talk." (Esboço / roteiro)
Agenda -- the list of topics you will cover, in order
"Here is the agenda for today's session." (Pauta / agenda)
Transition -- a smooth move from one part of a talk to the next
"That is a good transition to my next point." (Transição)
Emphasize -- to give special importance to a point
"I would like to emphasize this result." (Enfatizar)
Recap -- a short repeat of the main points at the end
"Let me recap the three key results." (Recapitulação / resumo)
Takeaway -- the most important idea to remember
"The main takeaway is that structure matters." (Lição principal)
Framework -- the basic structure or model behind your work
"We built the study on a clear framework." (Estrutura / modelo)
Overview -- a short general picture before the details
"First, a quick overview of the project." (Visão geral)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Outline
Agenda
Transition
Emphasize
Recap
Takeaway
Framework
Overview

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Patricia is about to give a talk at Abbott, so she plans a clear outline. She opens with a short overview and shows the agenda on the first slide. "Firstly," she says, "I will explain our research framework." After that, she makes a smooth transition: "Moving on, let me present the main findings." She wants to emphasize one important result, so she slows down and repeats it. At the end, she gives a clear recap of the key points. "To summarize," she says, "the main takeaway is that a clear structure helps both the speaker and the audience." Her colleague David tells her the talk was easy to follow, because the signposts showed exactly where she was at every moment.

1. What does Patricia show on the first slide?
A A short overview and the agenda.
B The final conclusion of the study.
C A list of her past publications.
2. How does she move from the framework to the findings?
A She stops the talk and starts again.
B She makes a smooth transition: "Moving on, let me present the main findings."
C She asks the audience to guess the results.
3. Why was the talk easy to follow?
A Because it was very short with no data.
B Because the signposts showed exactly where she was at every moment.
C Because she read everything from a script.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Signposting language

GRAMMAR

Em apresentações, usamos signposts (marcadores) para guiar o público pela estrutura da fala. / In presentations, we use signposts to guide the audience through the structure of the talk.

Function / FunçãoSignpost / MarcadorExample / Exemplo
Start / list a pointFirstly, / Secondly, / To begin with"Firstly, I will give you a quick overview."
Change topicMoving on, / Now, let me turn to"Moving on, let me present the findings."
Give emphasisI would like to emphasize ..."I would like to emphasize this result."
Close / recapTo summarize, / In short, ..."To summarize, structure is the foundation."

Key point / Ponto-chave: signposts não são o conteúdo -- são o mapa. Use Firstly / Secondly para listar, Moving on / Now let me turn to para mudar de tópico, I would like to emphasize para destacar, e To summarize / In short para fechar. Cuidado: usamos Firstly (não "At first") para listar o primeiro ponto.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let me start with a quick of the project."
"Here is the for today's session."
"I would like to this result."
"That is a good to my next point."
"Let me the three key results."
"The main is that structure matters."

Stage 2: Put the Presentation Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the parts of a structured presentation in the correct order.

?After that, move on to the main findings.
?First, give a short overview and present the agenda.
?Finally, recap the key points and share the main takeaway.
?Next, explain your framework and your methods.
?Then, discuss what the results mean.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Let me start with a quick overview and the agenda.
Vou começar com uma visão geral rápida e a pauta.
Firstly, let me explain our research framework.
Primeiro, deixe-me explicar nossa estrutura de pesquisa.
Moving on, let me present the main findings.
Seguindo em frente, vou apresentar os principais resultados.
To summarize, the main takeaway is structure.
Para resumir, a lição principal é a estrutura.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You are opening a talk and want to show the structure. You say:
A "Let me start with a quick overview and the agenda for today."
B "I do not know how to begin this talk."
C "Agenda overview the today for."
You want to change from your methods to your results. You say:
A "At first, I will present the findings now."
B "Moving on, let me present the main findings."
C "The results, but no transition."
You want to close the talk and leave one clear idea. You say:
A "For summarize, this is the end maybe."
B "To summarize, the main takeaway is that structure helps everyone."
C "I forgot what I wanted to say at the end."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are opening a short Abbott research talk. Structure it: give an overview, present the agenda, move through two sections with a clear transition, and close with a recap and one takeaway. Use at least four of today's words and one signpost (firstly, moving on, to summarize).

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1Let me start with a quick overview and the agenda.Vou começar com uma visão geral rápida e a pauta.
