2Firstly, let me explain our research framework.Primeiro, deixe-me explicar nossa estrutura de pesquisa.
3Moving on, let me present the main findings.Seguindo em frente, vou apresentar os principais resultados.
4I would like to emphasize this result.Gostaria de enfatizar este resultado.
5To summarize, the main takeaway is structure.Para resumir, a lição principal é a estrutura.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Explaining Methods & Processes

Describing how a study was done with the passive voice: the sample was analyzed, the data were obtained. New vocabulary: conduct, measure, procedure, protocol, obtain, standardize, dataset, analyze.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Conduct -- to carry out or run a study, test or experiment
"The study was conducted over six months." (Conduzir / realizar)
Measure -- to find the size, amount or value of something
"Each participant was measured at the start." (Medir)
Procedure -- a fixed series of steps done in a set order
"The same procedure was repeated every month." (Procedimento)
Protocol -- the official, written set of rules for a study
"The protocol was approved by the ethics committee." (Protocolo)
Obtain -- to get something, such as a result or data
"The results were obtained within forty-eight hours." (Obter)
Standardize -- to make a method or measurement the same for everyone
"Every measurement was standardized for accuracy." (Padronizar)
Dataset -- a complete, organized collection of data
"The dataset was reviewed by a statistician." (Conjunto de dados)
Analyze -- to examine data carefully in order to understand it
"The samples were analyzed in the central laboratory." (Analisar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Conduct
Measure
Procedure
Protocol
Obtain
Standardize
Dataset
Analyze

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the team meeting, Patricia explains how a six-month study was conducted. First, the protocol was approved by the ethics committee. Then two hundred participants were recruited, and each one was measured at the start. The same procedure was repeated every month, so nothing changed between visits. The blood samples were collected by trained nurses and stored carefully. After that, the samples were analyzed in the central laboratory, and the results were obtained within forty-eight hours. To keep the work reliable, every measurement was standardized, so the same method was used for every patient. Finally, the complete dataset was reviewed by an independent statistician before the figures were published.

1. What was done before any work began?
A The results were published.
B The protocol was approved by the ethics committee.
C The dataset was reviewed.
2. Why was every measurement standardized?
A So the same method was used for every patient.
B So the study could finish faster.
C So fewer patients were needed.
3. Who reviewed the complete dataset?
A The trained nurses.
C The participants.
B An independent statistician.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The passive voice

GRAMMAR

Para explicar um método, usamos a voz passiva: o foco fica na ação, não em quem a fez. / To explain a method, we use the passive voice: the focus is on the action, not on who did it.

Function / FunçãoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Singularwas + past participle"The sample was analyzed."
Pluralwere + past participle"The data were obtained."
Negativewas/were + not + p.p."The data were not changed."
Say who (optional)... by + agent"It was reviewed by a statistician."

Key point / Ponto-chave: A passiva é was (singular) ou were (plural) + o past participle (analyzed, obtained, collected). Ela coloca o foco na ação e no método, não na pessoa. Em uma seção de métodos, geralmente omitimos quem fez a ação. Lembre: data é plural, então use were.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The study was over six months."
"The was approved by the ethics committee."
"The same was repeated every month."
"The samples were in the central laboratory."
"The results were within forty-eight hours."
"The complete was reviewed by a statistician."

Stage 2: Put the Method in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of describing a method in the correct order.

?After that, describe how the samples were collected and measured.
?First, say that the protocol was approved.
?Finally, state that the results were obtained and reviewed.
?Next, explain how the participants were recruited.
?Then, mention how the dataset was analyzed.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

The study was conducted over six months.
O estudo foi conduzido ao longo de seis meses.
The samples were collected and analyzed in the lab.
As amostras foram coletadas e analisadas no laboratório.
The results were obtained within forty-eight hours.
Os resultados foram obtidos em até quarenta e oito horas.
Every measurement was standardized.
Cada medição foi padronizada.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You explain how the samples were examined in the lab. You say:
A "The samples were analyzed in the laboratory."
B "The samples analyzed in the laboratory."
C "The samples is analyze in the laboratory."
You explain how the data were collected. You say:
A "The data was obtain from the patients."
B "The data were obtained from the patients."
C "The data the patients obtained were."
You explain that the study followed official rules. You say:
A "The rules of study was a protocol thing."
B "The protocol was approved before the study began."
C "The protocol approve the study before begin."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are explaining the method of one of your own Abbott studies. In the passive voice, say how the study was conducted, how the participants were measured, how the samples were analyzed, and how the results were obtained. Use at least four of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1The study was conducted over six months.O estudo foi conduzido ao longo de seis meses.
2The protocol was approved before the study began.O protocolo foi aprovado antes de o estudo começar.
3The samples were collected and analyzed in the lab.As amostras foram coletadas e analisadas no laboratório.
4Every measurement was standardized.Cada medição foi padronizada.
5The results were obtained within forty-eight hours.Os resultados foram obtidos em até quarenta e oito horas.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Cause, Effect & Correlation

Talking about cause and effect with the first conditional, and telling correlation apart from causation. New vocabulary: correlation, causation, variable, outcome, threshold, significant, attribute, confound.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Association -- a link where two things tend to change together
"There was a strong association between dose and recovery." (Associação)
Causation -- when one thing directly produces or causes another
"An association is not the same as causation." (Causalidade)
Determinant -- a factor that can change and shape the result
"Age is another determinant we must consider." (Determinante / fator)
Consequence -- the result or effect that follows from a cause
"A higher dose was linked to a better consequence for recovery." (Consequência)
Threshold -- the level at which an effect starts to appear
"Above a certain threshold, the effect became visible." (Limiar)
Meaningful -- large or clear enough to be important and trusted
"The result was meaningful after we controlled for age." (Significativo / relevante)
Attribute -- to say that a result is caused by a particular thing
"Part of the effect can be attributed to age." (Atribuir)
Confound -- when a hidden variable confuses the link between cause and effect
"Age may confound the link between dose and recovery." (Confundir / distorcer)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Association
Causation
Determinant
Consequence
Threshold
Meaningful
Attribute
Confound

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the results meeting, Patricia explains the link in the data. There was a strong correlation between a higher dose and a better outcome: when the dose went up, the recovery score also went up. But she is careful, because a correlation is not causation. The patients on the higher dose were also younger, so age is a confounding variable. Part of the effect can be attributed to age, not only to the dose. Her plan is clear. If the team runs a second trial, they will control for age from the start. And if the correlation stays strong after they control for that variable, the result will be much more significant -- above the threshold where the effect really matters. Only then can she say, with confidence, that the dose has a real effect.

1. What did the data show between dose and outcome?
A A strong correlation: a higher dose was linked to a better outcome.
B Proof that the dose caused the recovery.
C No link at all.
2. Why is the correlation not proof of causation?
A Because the sample was too small.
B Because age is a confounding variable: the higher-dose patients were younger.
C Because the study was too short.
3. What will the team do in the second trial?
A Use a higher dose for everyone.
C Stop measuring recovery.
B Control for age from the start.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The first conditional

GRAMMAR

Para falar de causa e resultado provável, usamos a primeira condicional: if + presente (a causa) + will + verbo (o resultado). / To talk about a cause and its likely result, we use the first conditional: if + present (the cause) + will + verb (the result).

Function / FunçãoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Cause / Causaif + present"If the dose is higher, ..."
Effect / Resultadowill + verb"..., the outcome will improve."
Negative / Negativowill + not + verb"..., the result will not be reliable."
Question / PerguntaWill + ... + if + present"Will it be significant if we control for age?"

Key point / Ponto-chave: A primeira condicional é if + presente (a causa) + will + verbo (o resultado provável). Depois de if, use o presente, nunca will (não se diz if we will control, e sim if we control). Lembre também: uma correlation mostra que duas coisas andam juntas, mas não prova causation -- uma variável escondida pode confundir o resultado.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"There was a strong between the dose and recovery."
"An association is not proof of ."
"Age is a confounding we must control for."
"If we control for age, the result will be ."
"Part of the effect can be to age."
"Above a certain , the effect becomes significant."

Stage 2: Put the Argument in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a cause-and-effect argument in the correct order.

?After that, point to a possible confounding variable.
?First, state the correlation you found in the data.
?Finally, name the threshold where the effect becomes significant.
?Next, explain that correlation is not the same as causation.
?Then, say what you will control for in the next trial.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

There was a strong correlation between the dose and the outcome.
Havia uma forte correlação entre a dose e o resultado.
Correlation is not the same as causation.
Correlação não é o mesmo que causalidade.
If we control for age, we will know the truth.
Se controlarmos a idade, saberemos a verdade.
Part of the effect can be attributed to age.
Parte do efeito pode ser atribuída à idade.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

Two things move together in your data, but you are not sure one causes the other. You say:
A "There is a correlation, but it is not proof of causation."
B "The dose causes the recovery, for sure."
C "There is no link in the data."
You want to predict a result from a cause. You say:
A "If we will control for age, we know the truth."
B "If we control for age, we will know the truth."
C "If we controlling age, we will knowing the truth."
You warn that a hidden factor may explain the result. You say:
A "There is no other reason for the result."
B "A confounding variable may explain part of the effect."
C "The result is significant because it is large."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are presenting a finding from one of your own Abbott studies. State the correlation, explain why it is not causation, name a confounding variable, and use a first conditional to say what you will control for in the next trial. Use at least four of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1There was a strong correlation between the dose and the outcome.Havia uma forte correlação entre a dose e o resultado.
2Correlation is not the same as causation.Correlação não é o mesmo que causalidade.
3A confounding variable may explain the result.Uma variável de confusão pode explicar o resultado.
4If we control for age, we will know the truth.Se controlarmos a idade, saberemos a verdade.
5If the effect passes the threshold, it will be significant.Se o efeito passar do limiar, será significativo.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Networking at Conferences

Professional small talk and the follow-up: starting conversations, building rapport and keeping in touch after a conference. New vocabulary: colleague, acquaintance, mingle, rapport, exchange, contact, collaboration, business card.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Colleague -- a person you work with, often in the same field
"I would like you to meet a colleague from my lab." (Colega)
Acquaintance -- a person you know slightly, not a close friend
"She started as an acquaintance and became a research partner." (Conhecido(a))
Mingle -- to move around a room and talk to many different people
"During the coffee break, I like to mingle with new people." (Circular / socializar)
Rapport -- a friendly, comfortable connection between people
"We built a good rapport after just five minutes." (Sintonia / boa relação)
Exchange -- to give and receive something, such as contact details
"Before we left, we exchanged email addresses." (Trocar)
Contact -- a person you can reach later, useful for your work
"He is a valuable contact in the nutrition field." (Contato)
Collaboration -- the act of working together on a shared project
"The conversation led to a collaboration between our teams." (Colaboração / parceria)
Business card -- a small card with your name, role and contact details
"Here is my business card -- let us keep in touch." (Cartão de visita)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Colleague
Acquaintance
Mingle
Rapport
Exchange
Contact
Collaboration
Business card

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the conference, Patricia uses the coffee break to mingle. She walks up to a small group and opens with a question: "What did you think of the keynote?" Soon she is talking to Mark, a colleague from the same field. They quickly build a good rapport, because they are both interested in nutrition research. Mark was only an acquaintance before, but now there could be a real collaboration between their teams. Before they leave, they exchange business cards, and Patricia adds Mark as an important contact. "Here is my business card," she says. "It was great to meet you -- let's keep in touch." The next day, she sends a short follow-up message to keep the connection warm.

1. How does Patricia open the conversation?
A With a closed yes/no question.
B With an open question about the keynote.
C By giving a formal presentation.
2. What do Patricia and Mark do before they leave?
A They exchange business cards.
B They cancel the conference.
C They never speak again.
3. What does Patricia do the next day?
A She forgets about Mark.
C She asks for a refund.
B She sends a short follow-up message.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Small talk & the follow-up

GRAMMAR

Para fazer networking, abrimos com perguntas, construimos rapport e fechamos com frases educadas. / To network, we open with questions, build rapport, and close with polite fixed phrases.

Function / FunçãoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Open (Wh- question)What / How + ...?"What did you think of the keynote?"
Open (yes/no + follow-up)Are you...? + a real question"Are you presenting? What is it about?"
Build rapportshow interest + follow up"We work in the same field! Tell me more."
Close politelyfixed phrase"It was great to meet you. Let's keep in touch."

Key point / Ponto-chave: Abra com perguntas abertas (What / How), que pedem mais do que sim/não; construa rapport fazendo follow-up sobre o que a pessoa diz; e feche com frases fixas educadas antes de trocar contatos. Uma pergunta fechada (Do you like it?) costuma matar a conversa.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"During the break, I like to with new people."
"We built a good after just five minutes."
"Before we left, we decided to business cards."
"He is now a valuable in my field."
"The conversation led to a between our teams."
"Here is my -- let's keep in touch."

Stage 2: Put the Networking Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of networking at a conference in the correct order.

?After that, ask about their work and build rapport.
?First, walk up to a small group during the break and mingle.
?Finally, send a short follow-up message within two days.
?Next, start the conversation with a comment about the talk.
?Then, before you leave, exchange contact details and business cards.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Hi, I'm Patricia. What did you think of the keynote?
Oi, sou a Patricia. O que você achou da palestra principal?
We work in the same field -- tell me more about your study.
Nós trabalhamos na mesma área -- conte-me mais sobre o seu estudo.
Here is my business card. Shall we exchange details?
Aqui está meu cartão de visita. Vamos trocar contatos?
It was great to meet you. Let's keep in touch.
Foi ótimo conhecer você. Vamos manter contato.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to start a real conversation with a stranger after a talk. You say:
A "Hi, I'm Patricia. What did you think of the keynote?"
B "Did you like it? Yes or no?"
C "Keynote you the of think?"
The conversation is ending and you want to stay in contact. You say:
A "Can we change our business cards?"
B "It was great to meet you. Shall we exchange business cards?"
C "Goodbye, never talk again."
The day after the conference, you write a follow-up. You say:
A "I forgot who you are."
B "I really enjoyed our conversation -- maybe there's room for a collaboration."
C "Send money please."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you meet a new colleague at an Abbott conference. Open the conversation with a question, build rapport by finding something in common, and close by exchanging business cards. Then describe the follow-up message you would send the next day. Use at least four of today's words (mingle, rapport, exchange, contact, collaboration, business card).

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1Hi, I'm Patricia. What did you think of the keynote?Oi, sou a Patricia. O que você achou da palestra principal?
2We work in the same field -- tell me more about your study.Nós trabalhamos na mesma área -- conte-me mais sobre o seu estudo.
3Here is my business card. Shall we exchange details?Aqui está meu cartão de visita. Vamos trocar contatos?
4It was great to meet you. Let's keep in touch.Foi ótimo conhecer você. Vamos manter contato.
5I really enjoyed our conversation -- maybe there's room for a collaboration.Gostei muito da nossa conversa -- talvez haja espaço para uma colaboração.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Review -- Conference Presentation

Bringing Block 3 together into one talk: structure, data, methods and cause-effect, plus the language of delivery. New vocabulary: plenary, audience, deliver, engage, pacing, rehearse, critique, slide.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Plenary -- a large session that all the attendees join together
"My talk is the opening plenary of the symposium." (Sessão plenária)
Audience -- the group of people listening to your presentation
"I always look at the audience, not at my notes." (Plateia / público)
Deliver -- to give a talk or presentation to an audience
"I will deliver a twenty-minute talk on glycemic control." (Apresentar / proferir)
Engage -- to catch and keep the attention of your audience
"I ask a short question to engage the audience." (Envolver / cativar)
Pacing -- the speed and rhythm at which you speak
"Good pacing means I pause after each key number." (Ritmo da fala)
Rehearse -- to practise your talk before you give it for real
"I rehearse my talk two or three times the night before." (Ensaiar)
Critique -- honest, useful comments on how to improve your work
"My coach gave me a helpful critique after the rehearsal." (Crítica construtiva)
Slide -- one screen or page of your presentation deck
"On the next slide, you can see the main chart." (Slide / lâmina)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Plenary
Audience
Deliver
Engage
Pacing
Rehearse
Critique
Slide

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Patricia is ready to deliver the opening plenary at the Abbott symposium. The night before, she goes through every slide and rehearses the whole talk. Firstly, she explains the framework and the methods: the samples were analyzed in the central lab. Moving on, she describes the trends -- the main marker showed a steady increase, reached a peak, and then a plateau. Then she discusses the cause: the result is correlated with the new treatment, but she is careful to say that correlation is not causation. To keep the audience with her, she asks a short question and works on her pacing, pausing after each key number. Her coach David listens to the rehearsal and gives her an honest critique: the structure is clear, but she should slow down on the methods slide and engage the audience more during the results. She decides to rehearse it twice more before the big day.

1. What does Patricia do the night before her talk?
A She goes through every slide and rehearses the whole talk.
B She writes a brand-new study from scratch.
C She cancels the plenary session.
2. How is she careful about the cause of the result?
A She says the treatment definitely causes the result.
B She says the result is correlated with the treatment, but correlation is not causation.
C She ignores the cause completely.
3. What does David's critique tell her to improve?
A To make the structure less clear.
B To slow down on the methods slide and engage the audience more during the results.
C To remove all the data from the talk.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The full talk, four tools

GRAMMAR

Esta é uma aula de revisão: não há gramática nova. Você reúne as quatro ferramentas do Bloco 3 numa só fala. / This is a review lesson: no new grammar. You combine the four tools of Block 3 in a single talk.

Tool / FerramentaLanguage / LinguagemExample / Exemplo
Structure / EstruturaFirstly, / Moving on, / To summarize"Firstly, let me explain the methods."
Data / Dadosincrease, peak, plateau, proportion"The marker reached a peak and then a plateau."
Methods / MétodosPassive: was/were + past participle"The samples were analyzed in our lab."
Cause & Effectcorrelated with ... but not causation"It is correlated with the treatment, but correlation is not causation."

Key point / Ponto-chave: uma boa fala usa as quatro ferramentas. A estrutura guia o público, os dados mostram o quadro, a voz passiva dá credibilidade aos métodos, e a linguagem de causa-efeito mantém você honesta. / A good talk uses all four. The structure guides the audience, the data shows the picture, the passive gives credibility to the methods, and cause-effect language keeps you honest.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I will a twenty-minute talk in the opening session."
"On the next , you can see the main chart."
"Good means I pause after each key number."
"I ask a short question to the audience."
"I always my talk the night before."
"After the rehearsal, my coach gave me an honest ."

Stage 2: Put the Conference Talk in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the parts of a full conference talk in the correct order.

?Then, discuss the cause and effect behind the results.
?First, open with an overview and present the agenda.
?Finally, recap the key points and deliver one clear takeaway.
?Next, explain your framework and your methods.
?After that, describe the main trends in the data.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Welcome to this plenary session. Let me give you a quick overview.
Bem-vindos a esta sessão plenária. Deixem-me dar uma visão geral rápida.
The samples were analyzed using a standard protocol.
As amostras foram analisadas usando um protocolo padrão.
The result is correlated with the treatment, but correlation is not causation.
O resultado está correlacionado com o tratamento, mas correlação não é causação.
To summarize, the main takeaway is that structure builds trust.
Para resumir, a lição principal é que a estrutura gera confiança.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to explain how the data was collected, in a credible way. You say:
A "Somebody did something with the samples, I think."
B "The samples were analyzed in our central lab, and a standard protocol was followed."
C "The lab the samples in analyzed."
A reviewer says two lines move together. You want to stay honest. You say:
A "They are correlated, but correlation is not causation."
B "They move together, so one definitely causes the other."
C "I have no idea what the lines mean."
You want feedback to improve before the symposium. You say to your coach:
A "Please only tell me the talk is perfect."
B "Could you give me an honest critique of my pacing and delivery?"
C "Do not watch my rehearsal, please."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are delivering the opening plenary at the Abbott symposium. Give a short, complete talk: open with structure, describe one trend in your data, explain a method with the passive, discuss the cause carefully (correlation, not causation), and close with a recap and one takeaway. Use at least four of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Welcome to this plenary session. Let me give you a quick overview.Bem-vindos a esta sessão plenária. Deixem-me dar uma visão geral rápida.
2The samples were analyzed using a standard protocol.As amostras foram analisadas usando um protocolo padrão.
3The marker showed a steady increase and then a plateau.O marcador mostrou um aumento constante e depois um platô.
4The result is correlated with the treatment, but correlation is not causation.O resultado está correlacionado com o tratamento, mas correlação não é causação.
5To summarize, the main takeaway is that structure builds trust.Para resumir, a lição principal é que a estrutura gera confiança.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Abstracts & Papers

Writing up research in an abstract and a paper, using the present perfect for findings and the past simple for methods. New vocabulary: synopsis, prediction, results, peer review, citation, manuscript, submission, journal.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Synopsis -- a short summary of a paper: aim, methods, results and conclusion
"The synopsis sums up the whole study in one paragraph." (Resumo / sinopse)
Prediction -- what you predict and then test in your study
"Our prediction was that a higher dose would improve recovery." (Previsão)
Results -- what your study discovered and reports
"Our results have shown a strong effect." (Resultados)
Peer review -- when other experts read and check your work before it is published
"The paper passed peer review after two rounds of comments." (Revisão por pares)
Citation -- a reference to another study you build on
"Each claim needs a citation to support it." (Citação / referência)
Manuscript -- the written paper you prepare before it is published
"We finished the manuscript and sent it to the journal." (Manuscrito / artigo)
Submission -- the act of sending your work to a journal for review
"The submission is due at the end of the month." (Submissão / envio)
Journal -- a publication where research papers appear after review
"The paper was accepted by a leading medical journal." (Revista científica / periódico)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Synopsis
Prediction
Results
Peer review
Citation
Manuscript
Submission
Journal

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Patricia is preparing the abstract of a new manuscript. An abstract is short, but every sentence does one job. The first sentence gives the background. The second states the hypothesis: she predicted that a higher dose would lead to a better outcome. Then come the methods, written in the past simple, because they are finished: the team recruited ninety patients and measured their recovery over six months. After the methods come the findings, and here the tense changes. She uses the present perfect, because the findings are new and still matter today: the study has shown a strong association, and the team have found that the effect is significant. Every claim is backed by a citation. The register is careful -- there is no "I think" and no "maybe". Once the abstract is ready, Patricia will send the submission to the journal, and peer review will begin.

1. Which tense is used for the methods, and why?
A The past simple, because the methods are finished.
B The present perfect, because they still matter.
C The future, because they will be repeated.
2. Why are the findings in the present perfect?
A Because they happened a long time ago.
B Because they are new and still matter today.
C Because they are only a prediction.
3. What happens after the abstract is ready?
A The study is repeated from the start.
C The findings are deleted.
B Patricia sends the submission to the journal and peer review begins.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past simple vs present perfect

GRAMMAR

Em um abstract, usamos o past simple para métodos terminados e o present perfect para achados que ainda importam. / In an abstract, we use the past simple for finished methods and the present perfect for findings that still matter.

Function / FunçãoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Method / Métodopast simple"We measured recovery over six months."
Finding / Achadohas/have + past participle"The study has shown a strong effect."
Finding (we) / Achado (nós)have + past participle"We have found a clear association."
Negative / Negativohave not + past participle"We have not found any side effects."

Key point / Ponto-chave: Use o past simple para métodos terminados (we recruited, we measured) e o present perfect (has/have + particípio passado) para achados que ainda valem (the study has shown, we have found). Cuidado com o particípio: é has shown, nunca has showed. No registro acadêmico, evite "I think" e "maybe": prefira "the results suggest".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The sums up the whole study in one paragraph."
"Our was that a higher dose would improve recovery."
"Each claim needs a to support it."
"After , the manuscript was accepted."
"We finished the and sent it to the journal."
"The paper was accepted by a leading medical ."

Stage 2: Put the Abstract in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of an abstract in the correct order.

?After that, describe the methods in the past simple.
?First, give the background and the gap your study fills.
?Finally, give the conclusion and explain why it matters.
?Next, state the aim and the hypothesis you tested.
?Then, report the findings in the present perfect.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

The abstract sums up the aim, the methods and the findings.
O resumo sintetiza o objetivo, os métodos e os achados.
The study has shown a strong association.
O estudo mostrou uma forte associação.
We measured recovery over six months.
Medimos a recuperação ao longo de seis meses.
After peer review, the manuscript was accepted by the journal.
Após a revisão por pares, o manuscrito foi aceito pela revista.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You report a finding that is new and still matters. You write:
A "The study has shown a strong association."
B "The study has showed a strong association."
C "I think the study is good, maybe."
You describe a finished method. You write:
A "We have measured recovery over six months."
B "We measured recovery over six months."
C "We are measuring recovery over six months."
You want to support a claim in your paper. You add:
A "Everyone knows this is true."
B "A citation to an earlier study."
C "My personal opinion."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are writing the abstract of one of your own Abbott studies. Give the background and the hypothesis, describe the methods in the past simple, report the findings in the present perfect, and add a short conclusion. Use at least four of today's words.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1The abstract sums up the aim, the methods and the findings.O resumo sintetiza o objetivo, os métodos e os achados.
2We measured recovery over six months.Medimos a recuperação ao longo de seis meses.
3The study has shown a strong association.O estudo mostrou uma forte associação.
4Every claim is backed by a citation.Cada afirmação é sustentada por uma citação.
5After peer review, the manuscript was accepted by the journal.Após a revisão por pares, o manuscrito foi aceito pela revista.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Leading a Research Discussion

Chairing a meeting in English: opening the discussion, inviting contributions, keeping the group on track and wrapping up with clear action points. New vocabulary: facilitate, objective, contribution, redirect, allocate, action point, minutes, wrap up.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Facilitate -- to help a group have a good discussion and reach decisions
"As the chair, I facilitate the meeting so everyone can speak." (Facilitar / conduzir)
Objective -- the clear goal or aim of a meeting or discussion
"The objective today is to agree on the next steps." (Objetivo / meta)
Contribution -- an idea or comment a person adds to a discussion
"Thank you, Maria -- that is a very useful contribution." (Contribuição)
Redirect -- to bring a discussion back to the topic when it goes off track
"Let me redirect us back to the main objective." (Redirecionar / retomar)
Allocate -- to give a task or resource to a specific person
"Let's allocate the literature review to David." (Atribuir / designar)
Action point -- a specific task someone must do after the meeting
"The first action point is to recruit ten more patients." (Item de ação / tarefa)
Minutes -- the official written notes of what was decided in a meeting
"Could you take the minutes for today's discussion?" (Ata da reunião)
Wrap up -- to bring a meeting to a close by summarizing the decisions
"Let's wrap up: here are our three action points." (Encerrar / finalizar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Facilitate
Objective
Contribution
Redirect
Allocate
Action point
Minutes
Wrap up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Patricia is chairing a research meeting at Abbott. She opens clearly: "Let's start. The objective today is to agree on the next steps." As the chair, she facilitates the discussion instead of doing all the talking. She invites each person by name, so even the quieter colleagues make a contribution. When David goes off topic, she redirects the group politely: "That's a fair point, but let's come back to that later." As decisions are made, she allocates each action point to a specific person, with a deadline. Finally, she wraps up: she summarizes the decisions and confirms that someone is taking the minutes. A good chair turns a long meeting into a short list of clear tasks.

1. How does Patricia open the meeting?
A By doing all the talking herself.
B By stating the objective clearly.
C By ending the meeting at once.
2. What does Patricia do when David goes off topic?
A She redirects the group politely.
B She tells him he is wrong.
C She ignores everyone.
3. What does Patricia do at the end of the meeting?
A She leaves without a summary.
C She starts a new study.
B She wraps up and confirms the minutes.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Chairing a discussion

GRAMMAR

Para liderar uma discussão, usamos frases fixas para cada função: abrir, convidar, redirecionar e encerrar. / To lead a discussion, we use fixed phrases for each function: open, invite, redirect and close.

Function / FunçãoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Open the meetingLet's start by + -ing"Let's start by looking at the numbers."
Invite a contributionWhat are your thoughts on...?"What are your thoughts on the sample?"
Redirect politelyacknowledge + Let's come back"Fair point, but let's come back to that."
Wrap upTo sum up,..."To sum up, we have three action points."

Key point / Ponto-chave: O bom facilitador tem uma frase pronta para cada função: abra com o objective, convide cada pessoa pelo nome, redirecione com educação (reconheça e depois retome), atribua cada action point e encerre com um resumo claro. Rejeitar uma ideia de forma seca (That's wrong) faz as pessoas pararem de contribuir.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"As the chair, I the meeting so everyone can speak."
"The today is to agree on the next steps."
"Thank you, Maria -- that is a very useful ."
"Let me us back to the main objective."
"Let's the literature review to David."
"Could you take the for today's discussion?"

Stage 2: Put the Chairing Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of chairing a research discussion in the correct order.

?After that, if someone goes off topic, redirect the discussion politely.
?First, open the meeting and state the objective in one clear sentence.
?Finally, wrap up: summarize the decisions and make sure someone takes the minutes.
?Next, invite each person to make a contribution, and bring in the quieter voices.
?Then, as decisions are made, allocate each action point to a specific person.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Let's start. The objective today is to agree on the next steps.
Vamos começar. O objetivo de hoje é concordar com os próximos passos.
Maria, what are your thoughts on this?
Maria, qual é a sua opinião sobre isso?
That's a fair point, but let's come back to that later.
É uma observação justa, mas vamos retomar isso depois.
To sum up, we have three action points. I'll take the minutes.
Resumindo, temos três itens de ação. Eu farei a ata.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You need to open the meeting and tell everyone the goal. You say:
A "Let's start. The objective today is to agree on the next steps."
B "I don't know why we are here."
C "Steps next the agree on let's."
A colleague goes off topic and you want to stay polite. You say:
A "That's wrong. Next topic."
B "That's a fair point, but let's come back to that later."
C "Stop talking, please."
The meeting is ending and you want everyone to be clear. You say:
A "We did nothing today."
B "To sum up, we have three action points. I'll take the minutes."
C "Goodbye, figure it out."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are chairing a research meeting at Abbott. Open the meeting by stating the objective, invite one colleague to make a contribution, redirect an off-topic comment politely, allocate an action point, and wrap up. Use at least four of today's words (facilitate, objective, contribution, redirect, allocate, action point, minutes, wrap up).

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1Let's start. The objective today is to agree on the next steps.Vamos começar. O objetivo de hoje é concordar com os próximos passos.
2Maria, what are your thoughts on this?Maria, qual é a sua opinião sobre isso?
3That's a fair point, but let's come back to that later.É uma observação justa, mas vamos retomar isso depois.
4Let's allocate this action point to David, with a deadline.Vamos atribuir este item de ação ao David, com um prazo.
5To sum up, we have three action points. I'll take the minutes.Resumindo, temos três itens de ação. Eu farei a ata.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Cross-Cultural Scientific Communication

Working with global research teams: reading directness, respecting etiquette and norms, adapting your message to the context and showing sensitivity to avoid misunderstandings. New vocabulary: cultural, directness, etiquette, norm, adapt, misunderstanding, context, sensitivity.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Cultural -- connected to the shared habits, values and customs of a group or country
"We need to respect the cultural differences in our global team." (Cultural)
Directness -- how openly and clearly people say what they really think
"German colleagues often value directness." (Franqueza / objetividade)
Etiquette -- the accepted polite rules of behaviour in a group or situation
"Email etiquette is different from one country to another." (Etiqueta / boas maneiras)
Norm -- a usual, accepted way of behaving in a group or culture
"Using first names at once is the norm in some teams." (Norma / padrão)
Adapt -- to change your message or behaviour to fit the situation or listener
"A good communicator can adapt the message to each culture." (Adaptar)
Misunderstanding -- when people understand a message in different ways, often by mistake
"To avoid a misunderstanding, I repeat the key point clearly." (Mal-entendido)
Context -- the whole situation around a message that helps explain its meaning
"The same words can mean different things in a different context." (Contexto)
Sensitivity -- the ability to notice and respect other people's feelings and customs
"Cultural sensitivity helps you build trust with a global team." (Sensibilidade)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Cultural
Directness
Etiquette
Norm
Adapt
Misunderstanding
Context
Sensitivity

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Patricia works with a global research team at Abbott. She knows that the science is the same everywhere, but communication is not. She reads the directness of each colleague: her German colleagues say exactly what they think, while her colleagues in Japan are more indirect, so "it may be a little difficult" often means no. She respects the etiquette of each team and follows the local norm for names. When the meaning is not clear, she remembers that the same words can mean different things in a different cultural context, so she confirms understanding to avoid a misunderstanding. Most of all, she shows sensitivity: she assumes good intentions and is happy to adapt her message to the listener.

1. What does Patricia do about each colleague's directness?
A She treats everyone exactly the same.
B She reads it and adapts to each culture.
C She ignores cultural differences.
2. From the Japan team, what does "it may be a little difficult" often mean?
A It is a polite way of saying no.
B It means yes, no problem.
C It means they are angry.
3. How does Patricia avoid a misunderstanding?
A She says nothing at all.
C She speaks faster.
B She confirms understanding and adapts her message.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Softening across cultures

GRAMMAR

Para ser educado numa equipe global, suavizamos a fala com palavras tentativas (perhaps, could, may, might, a little) e perguntas educadas. / To be polite in a global team, we soften our message with tentative words (perhaps, could, may, might, a little) and polite questions.

Function / FunçãoDirectSofter / Mais educado
SuggestWe should redo it."Perhaps we could redo it."
RefuseNo, impossible."I'm afraid that may be a little difficult."
RequestMove the deadline."Would it be possible to move the deadline?"
ConfirmYou said yes."Just to make sure I understand, do you mean...?"

Key point / Ponto-chave: Em uma equipe global, suavize a mensagem com perhaps, could, may, might e a little, e confirme o entendimento (Just to make sure...) para mostrar sensitivity e evitar um misunderstanding. Adapte-se ao context: com colegas muito diretos, você também pode ser mais direto.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We need to respect the differences in our global team."
"German colleagues often value ."
"Using first names at once is the in some teams."
"A good communicator can the message to each culture."
"To avoid a , I repeat the key point clearly."
"Cultural helps you build trust with a global team."

Stage 2: Put the Cross-Cultural Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of communicating with a global team in the correct order.

?After that, adapt your own message to the cultural context of the listener.
?First, learn the basic etiquette of the team, such as how to use names.
?Finally, show sensitivity: assume good intentions and respect every cultural norm.
?Next, notice how direct people are, because in some cultures no is said softly.
?Then, repeat the key point in different words and confirm understanding.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Just to make sure I understand, do you mean the data is final?
Só para ter certeza de que entendi, você quer dizer que os dados são finais?
I see your point. Perhaps we could look at it differently.
Entendo o seu ponto. Talvez pudéssemos ver isso de outra forma.
I'm afraid that may be a little difficult for us.
Receio que isso possa ser um pouco difícil para nós.
I'm happy to adapt to whatever works best for the team.
Tenho prazer em me adaptar ao que funcionar melhor para a equipe.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You are not sure you understood a colleague's point. You say:
A "Just to make sure I understand, do you mean the data is final?"
B "You are not clear at all."
C "Final data the is mean you?"
You disagree with a colleague from a more indirect culture. You say:
A "No, that's wrong."
B "I see your point. Perhaps we could look at it differently."
C "Stop talking, please."
A colleague in Japan says "it may be a little difficult." You should:
A Push harder until they say yes.
B Read it as a polite no and not push.
C Ignore the comment completely.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are preparing for a global research call with colleagues in Japan, Germany and Brazil. Describe how you will read each team's directness, respect their etiquette, adapt your message to the context, and show sensitivity to avoid a misunderstanding. Use at least four of today's words (cultural, directness, etiquette, norm, adapt, misunderstanding, context, sensitivity).

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1Just to make sure I understand, do you mean the data is final?Só para ter certeza de que entendi, você quer dizer que os dados são finais?
2I see your point. Perhaps we could look at it differently.Entendo o seu ponto. Talvez pudéssemos ver isso de outra forma.
3In my culture, we often discuss the details first.Na minha cultura, costumamos discutir os detalhes primeiro.
4I'm afraid that may be a little difficult for us.Receio que isso possa ser um pouco difícil para nós.
5I'm happy to adapt to whatever works best for the team.Tenho prazer em me adaptar ao que funcionar melhor para a equipe.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

Capstone -- Abbott International Symposium

The final simulation: present your study, defend your data on a panel, synthesize and integrate the findings, and advocate for good science with strong closing remarks. New vocabulary: symposium, panel, present, synthesize, advocate, integrate, defend, closing remarks.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Symposium -- a formal meeting where experts present and discuss a specific topic
"I am giving a talk at the international symposium." (Simpósio)
Panel -- a group of experts who discuss a topic and answer questions publicly
"After my talk, the panel will ask some tough questions." (Painel / mesa de especialistas)
Present -- to show and explain your work clearly to an audience
"I will present my study, then take questions from the panel." (Apresentar)
Defend -- to support your data or position with strong reasons and evidence
"I can defend my data with the robust results from the trial." (Defender)
Synthesize -- to bring many ideas or findings together into one clear summary
"In the closing session, each speaker will synthesize the main findings." (Sintetizar)
Integrate -- to combine separate ideas or studies into one connected whole
"The final talk integrates the different studies into one message." (Integrar)
Advocate -- to speak publicly in support of an idea, policy or position
"As a researcher, I advocate for clear, honest science." (Defender / advogar por)
Closing remarks -- the final words of a talk that sum up the message and leave an impression
"In my closing remarks, I thanked the panel and gave one clear takeaway." (Considerações finais)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Symposium
Panel
Present
Defend
Synthesize
Integrate
Advocate
Closing remarks

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Today is the big day. Patricia will present her study on glycemic control at the Abbott International Symposium. First, she opens with the objective; next, she explains the methods; finally, she shows the key results. After her talk, she joins the expert panel. When a colleague challenges her sample, she stays calm: she acknowledges the point, then she will defend her data with the robust results, as a result, the findings are convincing. In the closing session, she has to synthesize the main ideas of the whole symposium and integrate the different studies into one clear message. In her closing remarks, she thanks the audience and continues to advocate for clear, honest science.

1. In what order does Patricia structure her talk?
A Objective, then methods, then results.
B Results first, then nothing else.
C She has no structure at all.
2. How does Patricia react when the panel challenges her sample?
A She gets angry and stops talking.
B She acknowledges the point, then defends the data with robust results.
C She agrees her study is wrong.
3. What does Patricia do in the closing session?
A She leaves before it starts.
C She reads only her own results again.
B She synthesizes and integrates the studies into one message.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Linking the whole talk

GRAMMAR

Um bom talk conecta as partes com linking language: signposting, causa/efeito, síntese e fechamento. / A strong talk connects its parts with linking language: signposting, cause/effect, synthesis and closing.

Job / FunçãoLinking languageExample
SignpostFirst / Next / Finally"First, I will present the objective."
Cause/effectas a result / therefore"The sample was large, as a result, the data is robust."
SynthesizeTo synthesize / To sum up"To synthesize, the studies integrate into one message."
CloseIn my closing remarks"In my closing remarks, thank you all."

Key point / Ponto-chave: Para o capstone, use linking language para integrate tudo: signpost as partes (first / next / finally), conecte causa e efeito (as a result / therefore), synthesize (to synthesize / to sum up) e feche com closing remarks. Assim você junta toda a sua jornada num talk fluido. / Use linking language to integrate everything into one smooth talk.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I am giving a talk at the international ."
"After my talk, the will ask some tough questions."
"I can my data with the robust results from the trial."
"In the closing session, each speaker will the main findings."
"The final talk will the different studies into one message."
"As a researcher, I for clear, honest science."

Stage 2: Put the Symposium Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of your symposium day in the correct order.

?After that, join the panel and defend your data, standing by your strong evidence.
?First, present your study clearly: objective, methods and key results.
?Finally, integrate the studies into one message and advocate for good science.
?Next, finish your talk with strong closing remarks and one clear message.
?Then, in the final session, synthesize the main findings of the whole symposium.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Speak slowly and clearly.

Good morning. Today I will present our study on glycemic control.
Bom dia. Hoje vou apresentar nosso estudo sobre controle glicêmico.
That is a fair point, but the robust results stand by themselves.
É uma observação justa, mas os resultados robustos falam por si.
To synthesize, the three studies all point to one clear message.
Para sintetizar, os três estudos apontam para uma mesma mensagem clara.
In my closing remarks, thank you, and let us advocate for good science.
Nas minhas considerações finais, obrigada, e vamos advogar por uma boa ciência.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You are opening your talk at the symposium. You say:
A "Good morning. Today I will present our study on glycemic control."
B "Study control my I present."
C "I do not know what to say."
The panel challenges your sample, but your results are strong. You say:
A "You are wrong, stop asking."
B "That is a fair point, but the robust results stand by themselves."
C "Maybe my study is no good."
In the closing session you must sum up all the studies. You say:
A "I will read my results one more time."
B "To synthesize, the studies integrate into one clear message."
C "There is nothing to say."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine your slot at the Abbott International Symposium. Describe your day: how you will present your study, defend your data on the panel, synthesize and integrate the findings, and advocate for good science in your closing remarks. Use at least four of today's words (symposium, panel, present, synthesize, advocate, integrate, defend, closing remarks).

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1Good morning. Today I will present our study on glycemic control.Bom dia. Hoje vou apresentar nosso estudo sobre controle glicêmico.
2That is a fair point, but the robust results stand by themselves.É uma observação justa, mas os resultados robustos falam por si.
3To synthesize, the three studies all point to one clear message.Para sintetizar, os três estudos apontam para uma mesma mensagem clara.
4Our findings integrate well with the other studies at the symposium.Nossas conclusões se integram bem com os outros estudos do simpósio.
5In my closing remarks, thank you, and let us advocate for good science.Nas minhas considerações finais, obrigada, e vamos advogar por uma boa ciência.

IN CLASS -- Select Lesson

01
Who Is Patricia Ruffo?
Diagnostic + Self-Introduction -- 27 slides
02
Holding the Floor
Opinions & Disagreement -- 28 slides
03
Smart Questions
Conference Discussions -- slides
04
Reporting & Updating
Team Presentations -- 28 slides
05
Abbott Science Summit
Full Simulation — 28 slides
06
Finding Common Ground
Diplomatic Negotiation: softening language, concessions and reaching agreement -- 28 slides
12
Describing Data, Trends & Charts
Reading the data story: comparatives and trend language to describe charts, increases, declines and peaks -- 30 slides
15
Review -- Conference Presentation
Block 3 review: structure a full conference talk, describe data, explain methods and cause-effect, and deliver under the spotlight -- 30 slides
16
Abstracts & Papers
The academic register: writing up research in an abstract and a paper, using the present perfect for findings and the past simple for methods -- 28 slides
17
Networking at Conferences
Professional small talk and the follow-up: starting conversations, building rapport and keeping in touch after a conference -- 30 slides
18
Leading a Research Discussion
Chairing a meeting in English: opening the discussion, inviting contributions, keeping the group on track and wrapping up with clear action points -- 30 slides
19
Cross-Cultural Scientific Communication
Working with global research teams: cultural norms, directness vs indirectness, etiquette, and how to adapt your message and avoid misunderstandings -- 30 slides
20
Capstone -- Abbott International Symposium
The final simulation: present your study, synthesize the findings, defend your data on a panel and integrate everything you learned -- bringing the whole journey together with strong closing remarks -- 30 slides
13
Explaining Methods & Processes
Describing how a study was done with the passive voice: the sample was analyzed, the data were obtained -- 28 slides
14
Cause, Effect & Correlation
Talking about cause and effect with the first conditional and telling correlation apart from causation -- 28 slides
09
Defending Your Data Under Pressure
Q&A under pressure: clarifying, conceding the small point and standing by your core result -- 28 slides
07
Making the Case
Persuasion and cause-and-effect language to build a convincing business argument -- 28 slides
08
Questioning Research
Critical appraisal and hedging: judging a study and questioning it politely -- 28 slides
10
The Scientific Debate
Review of Block 2: persuasion, questioning research and defending data in a mock panel debate -- 28 slides
11
Structuring a Scientific Presentation
Signposting: organizing a talk with firstly, moving on, and to summarize -- 28 slides

Materiais Complementares -- Aula 1

Lesson 1 — Who Is Patricia Ruffo?

TED Talk
The next outbreak? We're not ready -- Bill Gates

Vocabulário científico em velocidade moderada. Bill Gates apresenta dados de forma clara e estruturada -- ótimo modelo para apresentações científicas.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote 5 palavras novas.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
Science Friday (NPR) -- Nutrition & Health Episodes

Episódios sobre nutrição, saúde e pesquisa científica. Linguagem acessível com vocabulário técnico relevante para sua área.

Dica: ouça 15 minutos por dia no caminho para o trabalho.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Article
Nature News -- Opinion Section on Clinical Nutrition Research

Artigos de opinião sobre pesquisa em nutrição clínica. Prática de leitura acadêmica com argumentação científica -- diretamente relacionado ao seu foco.

Dica: leia um artigo e identifique como o autor estrutura seus argumentos.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Holding the Floor

TED Talk
Dare to Disagree -- Margaret Heffernan

Como o conflito produtivo leva a melhores decisões. Vocabulário rico em opinion frames e argumentação profissional -- diretamente relacionado ao tema da aula.

Dica: anote 3 opinion frames que a palestrante usa e tente replicá-las.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
Harvard Business Review IdeaCast -- Negotiation & Persuasion

Episódios sobre como apresentar argumentos, negociar e persuadir em contextos corporativos. Linguagem formal e opinion frames usados em reuniões reais.

Dica: ouça um episódio e identifique como os convidados concordam e discordam.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Article
The Economist -- Debate Section

Artigos de debate com posições opostas sobre temas atuais. Excelente para observar como argumentos e contra-argumentos são estruturados em inglês formal.

Dica: leia um debate e identifique os opinion frames usados por cada lado.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Smart Questions

TED Talk
The surprising habits of original thinkers -- Adam Grant

Como pensadores originais questionam premissas e formulam perguntas inteligentes. Excelente modelo de como desafiar hipóteses com elegância e clareza.

Dica: observe como Adam Grant estrutura perguntas retóricas e tente replicá-las.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
Freakonomics Radio -- How to Ask Better Questions

Episódios sobre como formular perguntas melhores usando dados e pensamento crítico. Vocabulário de pesquisa e metodologia em linguagem acessível.

Dica: ouça um episódio e anote 3 perguntas que usam vocabulary desta aula.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Article
Scientific American -- How Peer Review Works

Artigo sobre o processo de revisão por pares na ciência. Vocabulário acadêmico essencial como hypothesis, methodology, bias e peer-reviewed em contexto real.

Dica: leia o artigo e identifique as 10 palavras do vocabulário desta aula no texto.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Reporting & Updating

TED Talk
How to speak so that people want to listen -- Julian Treasure

Técnicas de apresentação e como estruturar uma fala para que a audiência preste atenção. Vocabulário de apresentações corporativas.

Dica: observe como Julian Treasure estrutura suas ideias e tente aplicar as técnicas na sua próxima apresentação.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
Manager Tools -- Project Update Communication

Episódios sobre como comunicar progresso, marcos e entregas de forma clara e profissional em reuniões corporativas.

Dica: ouça um episódio e anote as expressões usadas para relatar progresso e prazos.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Article
Harvard Business Review -- How to Give a Great Project Update

Artigos sobre boas práticas de comunicação de progresso em projetos, com foco em stakeholders e feedback.

Dica: leia o artigo e identifique as palavras do vocabulário desta aula no texto.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — Abbott Science Summit

TED Talk
Your body language may shape who you are -- Amy Cuddy

Como a linguagem corporal influência apresentações em conferências. Ótimo para preparação de keynotes e painéis.

Dica: observe as técnicas de linguagem corporal e pense em como aplicá-las na sua próxima apresentação em um simpósio.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
The TED Interview -- How to Give a Great Talk

Episódios sobre como preparar e entregar apresentações científicas impactantes em simpósios internacionais.

Dica: ouça um episódio e anote as expressões usadas para estruturar uma apresentação científica.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Article
Nature -- How to Network at Scientific Conferences

Guia prático de networking em conferências científicas, com dicas para delegados e palestrantes.

Dica: leia o artigo e identifique o vocabulário de networking e conferências que você aprendeu neste programa.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Finding Common Ground

Series
Suits -- any negotiation scene (Season 1)

Watch how lawyers disagree, make concessions and reach an agreement without being aggressive. Notice the softening language they use to stay polite under pressure.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three softening phrases (could, might, perhaps, that said) and one concession that you hear.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Diplomatic English: Disagreeing Politely"

A focused episode on softening your message in meetings: raising a reservation, making a concession and finding middle ground in English.

Record a 2-minute role-play: disagree with a colleague's idea, then propose a compromise using today's vocabulary.

Open ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "How to disagree politely in English"

A clear lesson on the modals and phrases that soften disagreement (could, might, I would say, perhaps). It directly reinforces today's diplomatic language.

After watching, write six sentences: three that raise a reservation politely and three that offer a concession.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Making the Case

Series
Mad Men -- any client pitch scene

Watch how the team builds a persuasive case: they present proof, explain the impact, and connect cause and effect to convince the client. Notice the structure of a strong argument.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one piece of proof and one cause-and-effect sentence (because, therefore, as a result) that you hear.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Making a Persuasive Business Case"

A focused episode on building an argument in English: stating a recommendation, presenting proof, and using cause-and-effect language to persuade a decision-maker.

Record a 2-minute pitch: recommend an investment, present your proof, and conclude with your rationale using today's vocabulary.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Linking words: cause and effect"

A clear lesson on the connectors that build an argument (because, therefore, as a result, lead to). It directly reinforces today's cause-and-effect language.

After watching, write six sentences: three with because (cause) and three with therefore or as a result (effect).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Questioning Research

Documentary
The Game Changers -- watch any 20-minute segment

A documentary full of bold nutrition claims. Practice your critical eye: notice when the data only suggests an effect, when the cohort is small, and when there may be conflict of interest.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two claims, then add a polite query and one limitation for each, using hedging (suggests, seems, may).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Science Vs -- any episode reviewing a health study

Each episode weighs the evidence behind a popular claim, naming the study design, cohort and limitations. A perfect model of balanced, hedged appraisal in natural English.

Record a 2-minute appraisal of the study they discuss: comment on the study design, raise one query, and give a verdict using plausible rather than proven.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"How to read a scientific paper critically" (any clear explainer)

A short guide to the language of appraisal: study design, cohort, conflict of interest, assumption and limitation. It directly reinforces today's vocabulary and the hedging used to question a study.

After watching, write six sentences: three that raise a query politely and three that name a limitation, using suggests, seems or may.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Defending Your Data Under Pressure

Series
The Crown -- any cross-examination or committee scene

Watch how confident speakers stay calm under hard questions: they recognize a fair point, then stand by their position without getting defensive. Notice the concede-then-defend pattern.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one rebuttal you hear and the moment the speaker recognizes a point before standing firm.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Handling Tough Questions in a Q&A"

A focused episode on staying calm under pressure: clarifying your point, conceding a fair criticism and standing by your main argument in English.

Record a 2-minute rebuttal: a reviewer attacks your data; recognize one point, then defend your robust result using today's vocabulary.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
TED-Ed / TED -- "How to defend your ideas under questioning"

A clear talk on answering critical questions calmly: clarify, recognize, give a rebuttal and stand by robust evidence. It directly reinforces today's pressure language.

After watching, write six sentences: three that concede a fair point and three that stand firm using "but", "however" or "that said".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — The Scientific Debate

Series
The Crown -- any cabinet or debate scene

Watch how speakers articulate a clear argument, hear a counterpoint, and rebut it without attacking the other person. Notice how the most persuasive speaker stays calm.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one persuasive argument and one counterpoint that you hear.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Intelligence Squared (IQ2) Debates

A real debate podcast: a moderator reads the motion, two sides argue, and the audience decides which case was more persuasive. Perfect to hear rebuttals and counterpoints in action.

Pick one episode. Note how each speaker rebuts a counterpoint with evidence, then say the argument again in your own words.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED-Ed -- "How to argue like an expert"

A short, clear video on how to build a valid, persuasive argument and how to rebut a counterpoint politely. It reinforces every skill from Block 2.

After watching, record a 2-minute mock debate: read a motion, give one argument with evidence, and rebut one counterpoint.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Structuring a Scientific Presentation

Talk
TED -- any well-structured science talk (e.g. Hans Rosling)

Watch how a great speaker opens with an overview, signposts each section, and closes with a clear takeaway. Notice how the structure keeps you with the speaker the whole time.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three signposts (firstly, moving on, to summarize) and the one takeaway you remember.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Signposting Language for Presentations"

A focused episode on the phrases that structure a talk: opening with an outline, making transitions, emphasizing a point, and summarizing at the end.

Record a 2-minute mini-talk: open with an overview and agenda, then close with a recap using today's vocabulary.

Open ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Presentation language: signposting"

A clear lesson on the phrases that guide an audience through a presentation (firstly, moving on, to summarize). It directly reinforces today's structuring language.

After watching, write six sentences: two openings, two transitions, and two closings using today's signposts.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Describing Data, Trends & Charts

Documentary
Explained (Netflix) -- any data-driven episode

Watch how presenters describe trends, increases, declines and peaks on real charts, and how they compare groups with simple comparatives.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences that describe a trend (rose sharply, reached a peak, leveled off) and one comparison (higher than, the most).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Describing Trends and Charts in English"

A focused episode on the language of data: trend verbs, comparatives and the words you need to present a chart clearly in a meeting.

Record a 2-minute description of an imaginary line graph: introduce the axes, describe the trend, point out the peak and plateau, and give one proportion.

Open ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Describing graphs and trends in English"

A clear lesson on the vocabulary and grammar for describing data: increase, decline, peak, plateau, fluctuate, and the comparatives that compare two lines.

After watching, write six sentences about a chart: two with an increase, two with a decline, and two comparing groups (higher than, the highest).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Explaining Methods & Processes

Documentary
Explained (Netflix) -- any science or health episode

Watch how a documentary narrates how research is done: how a study was conducted, how samples were collected and how data were analyzed.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three passive sentences you hear (was conducted, were collected, was analyzed) and the process word in each.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Using the Passive Voice in English"

A focused episode on the passive voice and the language of processes: how to describe a procedure, a protocol and the steps of a method clearly.

Record a 2-minute explanation of a simple method using only the passive: how it was conducted, how the samples were measured, and how the data were analyzed.

Open ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The passive voice in English"

A clear lesson on forming the passive: was and were plus the past participle, and when to leave out the person who did the action.

After watching, write six passive sentences about a method, using procedure, protocol, dataset, conduct, obtain and analyze.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Cause, Effect & Correlation

Documentary
Connected (Netflix) -- "Surveillance" or any data-focused episode

Watch how a science documentary explains links in data: where there is a correlation, where there may be causation, and which variables could confound a result.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two claims and decide: is each one a correlation or real causation? Note any confounding variable.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Freakonomics Radio -- any episode on "correlation vs causation"

A clear, accessible discussion of why two things that move together do not prove that one causes the other, and how hidden variables can confound a finding.

Record a 2-minute summary of one example from the episode: state the correlation, name the confounding variable, and use a first conditional to say how it could be tested.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The first conditional"

A clear lesson on forming the first conditional: if + present for the cause, will + verb for the result, and why we never use will after if.

After watching, write six first-conditional sentences about your own research, using correlation, variable, outcome, threshold, significant and attribute.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Review: Conference Presentation

Talk
TED -- a full science keynote (e.g. Hans Rosling, "The best stats you've ever seen")

Watch a complete conference talk and notice the four blocks at work: the structure, the way the speaker describes the data, how the methods are explained, and how cause and effect are discussed honestly.

Watch with English subtitles. Note how the speaker engages the audience and controls pacing. Write down one phrase for each of the four blocks.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Delivering a Presentation with Confidence"

An episode on the delivery side of a talk: how to engage the audience, control your pacing, and handle questions and critique after you present.

Record a 3-minute conference talk that uses all four blocks, then listen back and give yourself an honest critique of your pacing.

Listen on Apple Podcasts ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "How to give a great presentation"

A clear lesson on putting a whole presentation together: opening, structure, delivering with good pacing, and engaging your audience from start to finish.

After watching, rehearse your symposium talk once more, focusing on the three points David gave Patricia: pacing, engaging the audience, and adding energy to your key point.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Abstracts & Papers

Documentary
The Bleeding Edge (Netflix) -- any segment on published medical research

Watch how medical findings move from a study to a published paper, and how peer review and citations shape what reaches patients.

Watch with English subtitles. Note one finding and rewrite it twice: once as a method (past simple) and once as a finding (present perfect).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Nature Podcast -- any episode discussing a new paper

Researchers explain a recent paper in clear English: the hypothesis, the methods, the findings and what peer review added before publication.

Record a 2-minute abstract of the paper they discuss: state the hypothesis, the methods in the past simple, and the findings in the present perfect.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"How to write a scientific abstract" -- any clear academic-writing tutorial

A short guide to the shape of an abstract: background, hypothesis, methods, findings and conclusion, with the tense choices that go with each part.

After watching, draft a six-sentence abstract for one of your own studies, using abstract, hypothesis, findings, citation, manuscript and journal.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Networking at Conferences

Series
The Office (US) -- any conference or party episode

Watch how characters mingle, make small talk, build rapport and exchange contacts. Notice the open questions that start conversations and the polite phrases that close them.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three open questions someone uses to start a conversation, and one polite way to end one.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Networking and Small Talk in English"

A focused episode on the language of networking: opening lines, building rapport, exchanging contacts and following up after an event.

Record a 1-minute networking conversation: open with a question, build rapport, and close by exchanging business cards.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "How to make small talk and network in English"

A clear lesson on the phrases for professional small talk: open questions, building rapport, exchanging contacts and the polite phrases that close a conversation.

After watching, write a short follow-up message to an imaginary contact: thank them, mention something specific, and suggest a collaboration.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Leading a Research Discussion

Series
The West Wing -- any senior staff meeting scene

Watch how the chair opens a meeting, invites contributions, keeps the discussion on track and closes with clear next steps. Notice the phrases used to redirect people politely.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one phrase used to open the meeting, one to invite an opinion, and one to bring the group back on track.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Chairing a Meeting in English"

A focused episode on the language of facilitation: opening the meeting, inviting contributions, redirecting off-topic talk, allocating action points and wrapping up.

Record yourself chairing a 1-minute meeting: open with the objective, invite one contribution, and wrap up with an action point.

Open ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "English for meetings: leading and chairing"
