A clear lesson on the phrases a chair uses to open, invite, redirect and close a discussion, with realistic meeting-room examples.

After watching, write the three action points from an imaginary meeting and say who each one is allocated to.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Cross-Cultural Scientific Communication

Series
The Diplomat -- any cross-cultural negotiation scene

Watch how characters from different countries read each other's directness, soften their messages and avoid misunderstandings under pressure. Notice the polite, tentative language.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one phrase used to soften a disagreement and one used to confirm understanding.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "Working Across Cultures" (Erin Meyer)

An episode on cultural differences in directness, etiquette and communication style at work, and how to adapt your message to a global team.

Record yourself describing one cultural norm you would adapt to when working with a colleague from another country.

Listen on HBR ↗

YouTube
Erin Meyer -- "The Culture Map" (talk on cross-cultural communication)

A clear talk on how directness, feedback and etiquette differ across cultures, with practical advice on adapting your message and avoiding misunderstandings.

After watching, note two phrases you could use to confirm understanding and soften a disagreement on a global call.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Capstone: Abbott International Symposium

Series
The Newsroom -- any panel or live-defense scene

Watch how a speaker presents a position, defends it under tough questions and synthesizes the argument into one clear message. Notice the signposting and the calm, confident tone.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one phrase used to defend a claim and one used to sum up an argument.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
The Tim Ferriss Show -- any episode on public speaking & defending ideas

An interview on how experts present clearly, handle hard questions and advocate for their ideas in public. Useful models for the panel and your closing remarks.

Record yourself synthesizing the main idea of the episode in three sentences, then add one closing remark.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED -- "How to give a killer presentation" / a strong scientific TED talk

A model of a complete talk: a clear opening, signposted parts, strong evidence and memorable closing remarks. Watch how the speaker integrates everything into one message.

After watching, plan your own 5-minute symposium talk: opening, key result, one panel answer and your closing remarks.

Watch on YouTube ↗

The Scientist Key Words The Code In Context Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
Lesson 1 -- Business English

Who Is
Patricia Ruffo?

B1+ -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Scientist

Let's Talk About You

"Tell me about your work at Abbott."
"What do you enjoy most about clinical research?"
Chapter 1: The Scientist

Your English Journey

"When do you need English the most at work?"
"What is the hardest part of speaking English in conferences?"
Chapter 2: Key Words

Your Professional
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for scientific self-introduction

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

To tell someone your name or present a topic
Introduce
To tell someone your name or present a topic
"Let me introduce myself."
To work together with someone on a project
Collaborate
To work together with someone on a project
"I collaborate with universities."
Having the duty to manage or control something
Responsible
Having the duty to manage or control something
"I am responsible for the research program."
At the present time; now
Currently
At the present time; now
"I am currently working on a new trial."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

Scientific studies that test treatments on people
Clinical Research
Scientific studies that test new treatments on people
"Clinical research is essential for new treatments."
Food components that provide health benefits beyond nutrition
Functional Ingredients
Food components that provide health benefits beyond nutrition
"We study functional ingredients for glycemic control."
Managing blood sugar levels in the body
Glycemic Control
Managing blood sugar levels in the body
"Glycemic control is critical for patients with diabetes."
A formal test of something new, especially a treatment
Trial
A formal test of something new, especially a medical treatment
"The trial showed promising results."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Introduce"Let me introduce myself."
Collaborate"I collaborate with universities."
Responsible"I am responsible for the program."
Currently"I am currently working on a trial."
Clinical Research"Clinical research is essential."
Functional Ingredients"We study functional ingredients."
Glycemic Control"Glycemic control is critical."
Trial"The trial showed promising results."
Chapter 3: The Code

Grammar Discovery

Look at the green words. What do they have in common?

She works at Abbott. She reviews data every day.
She has worked in research for twenty years.
Patricia collaborates with universities.
She has published several papers on glycemic control.
TenseUseExample
Present SimpleFacts / HabitsShe works at Abbott.
Present PerfectExperience / AchievementShe has published papers.
Present PerfectDuration to nowShe has worked for 20 years.
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I work here since 2015."
"I have worked here since 2015."

Use Present Perfect with "since" and "for" when the action continues to the present.

Practice

Fill in: Simple or Perfect?

"She ___works at Abbott every day."
"She ___has worked in research for twenty years."
"Patricia ___collaborates with universities regularly."
"She ___has published several papers."
"The trial ___shows promising results this month."
"I ___have been collaborating with Dr. Lee since 2020."
Chapter 4: In Context

Patricia at the
ASPEN Conference

Time to use your vocabulary in real professional situations.

Artifact

Conference Invitation

From: speakers@aspen-conference.org
To: patricia.ruffo@abbott.com
Subject: Your Presentation at ASPEN 2026

Dear Dr. Ruffo,

We are pleased to confirm your presentation at the ASPEN Nutrition Science Conference 2026 in Chicago.

Topic: Functional Ingredients and Glycemic Control: A Clinical Trial Update

Date: September 15, 2026

Time: 2:00 PM - 2:30 PM (Hall B)

Format: 20-minute presentation + 10-minute Q&A

Please confirm your attendance by June 30. We look forward to your research updates.

ASPEN Conference Committee

1. What is the topic of Patricia's presentation?
Functional Ingredients and Glycemic Control: A Clinical Trial Update
2. How long is the Q&A session?
10 minutes
3. When must Patricia confirm her attendance?
By June 30
Listening

Conference Introduction

Listen to a researcher introducing herself at a conference.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What is the speaker's name and role?
Dr. Patricia Ruffo, clinical nutritionist at Abbott
2. What is she currently working on?
Glycemic control with functional ingredients
3. How long has she been collaborating with universities?
Over ten years
Dialogue

Meeting Dr. Lee

L
Hi! I am Dr. Lee from Seoul National University. Are you presenting today?
P
Yes! Let me introduce myself. I am Patricia Ruffo, a clinical nutritionist at Abbott.
L
Nice to meet you! What is your presentation about?
P
I am currently working on glycemic control with functional ingredients. Our latest trial has shown very promising results.
L
That sounds fascinating! How long have you been in clinical research?
P
I have been in clinical research for over twenty years. I collaborate with several universities in Brazil.
L
Impressive! I would love to hear more. Are you free after your presentation?
P
Absolutely! I am responsible for the afternoon session, but I am free after four. Let us connect!
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What is Patricia currently working on?
Glycemic control with functional ingredients.
2. Where is Dr. Lee from?
Seoul National University.
3. When is Patricia free to meet Dr. Lee?
After four o'clock, when the afternoon session ends.
Listening

Q&A Session

Listen to a question from the audience after Patricia's presentation.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What does the audience member ask about?
The sample size and duration of the clinical trial
2. What verb tense does Patricia use to describe results?
Present perfect ("The trial has shown...")
Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
Someone at a conference asks: "What do you do?" Introduce yourself professionally.
"I am a clinical nutritionist at Abbott. I am responsible for glycemic control research."
Detective

Spot the Error

"I work here since 2005."
"I have worked here since 2005."
"She collaborate with many universities."
"She collaborates with many universities."
"I am currently work on a new trial."
"I am currently working on a new trial."
"She has publish several papers."
"She has published several papers."
"I am responsible of the research program."
"I am responsible for the research program."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. A researcher asks your name and role. What do you say?
"I am Patricia Ruffo. I am a clinical nutritionist at Abbott."
2. Someone asks what you are currently working on. Respond.
"I am currently working on glycemic control with functional ingredients."
3. A colleague asks how long you have been in research. Answer.
"I have been in clinical research for over twenty years."
4. Someone asks about your latest results. What do you say?
"Our latest trial has shown very promising results for glycemic control."
5. You want to network after a presentation. What do you say?
"I would love to discuss this further. Are you free after the session?"
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Conference Lobby

You are at the ASPEN conference lobby. Dr. Lee from Seoul approaches you. Introduce yourself, explain your current research, and mention one achievement.
introducecurrentlyclinical researchcollaboratetrial
Role-Play

Role-Play: Q&A Session

You just finished your presentation. A researcher asks about your trial methodology and results. Answer confidently using present simple and present perfect.
responsiblefunctional ingredientsglycemic control
Free Practice

Role-Play: Free Networking

You are at the conference dinner. A colleague asks about your career and research. Have a conversation. No keywords, no script. Just talk!

Use everything you learned today: your name, role, current work, achievements, and collaboration experience.

Reflection

Record Yourself

Introduce yourself as if you are at the ASPEN conference. Include: your name, your role at Abbott, what you are currently working on, and one achievement.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Scientist Badge Earned!

You can now introduce yourself confidently at any conference, Patricia.

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1
"Let me introduce myself. I am Patricia Ruffo from Abbott."
2
"I am currently working on glycemic control research."
3
"I have been collaborating with universities for over ten years."
4
"Our latest trial has shown very promising results."
5
"I would love to discuss this further."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can introduce myself professionally in English.
I know 8 scientific vocabulary words.
I can use Present Simple for facts and habits.
I can use Present Perfect for experience and duration.
I can read and understand a conference invitation.
I can have a professional conversation at a conference.
I can describe my research to a colleague.
I feel more confident about networking in English.
Lesson Complete

Day 1 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Holding the Floor -- Opinions & Disagreement

T
LESSON 1
